<div class="content-wrap">
	<div class="content">
		<div class="feature-island">
			<div class="feature-island-main-block">

	<h2 class="fi-main-block__title">
		<a class="post-block__title__link" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20231202000540/https://techcrunch.com/2023/12/01/23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry/">23andMe says hackers accessed &#8216;significant number&#8217; of files about users&#8217; ancestry</a>
	</h2>

<p class="fi-main-block__byline">
			<a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20231202000540/https://techcrunch.com/author/lorenzo-franceschi-bicchierai/">Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai</a>,&nbsp;			<a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20231202000540/https://techcrunch.com/author/zack-whittaker/">Zack Whittaker</a>	</p>

<div class="post-block__content">

<div class="river-byline">

<div class="post-block__content">
		A new report by the Financial Times says X will now turn to small and medium-sized advertisers to shore up revenue after the company&#8217;s owner Elon Musk alienated big brands fleeing X over antisem	</div>

var tc_app_data = {"ads":{"gemini_enabled":false,"gemini_codes":{"position_4":{"mobile":"ae64471c-14c2-4457-8e9b-a5190206eafd","desktop":"b2018d9e-dedc-4e85-8823-b06917beb892"},"position_8":{"mobile":"619392a1-b497-4aa9-b683-24aab7246c16","desktop":"05c0b21d-e742-494d-ac59-474e3feaa4b8"}}},"assetURI":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/themes\/techcrunch-2017","breaking":"0","live_event":"0","livestream":"","feature_islands":{"homepage":[{"id":2636845,"date":"2023-12-01T14:43:04","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:43:04","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636845"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:19:36","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:19:36","slug":"23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry\/","title":{"rendered":"23andMe says hackers accessed &#8216;significant number&#8217; of files about users&#8217; ancestry"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/www.sec.gov\/ix?doc=\/Archives\/edgar\/data\/1804591\/000119312523287449\/d242666d8ka.htm\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission<\/a> published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer base. <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.23andme.com\/news-releases\/news-release-details\/23andme-reports-fy2023-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-financial\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">According to the company&#8217;s most recent annual earnings report<\/a>, 23andMe has \u201cmore than 14 million customers worldwide,\u201d which means 0.1% is around 14,000.<\/p>\n<p>But the company also said that by accessing those accounts, the hackers were also able to access \u201ca significant number of files containing profile information about other users\u2019 ancestry that such users chose to share when opting in to 23andMe\u2019s DNA Relatives feature.\u201d<\/p>\n<p>The company did not specify what that \u201csignificant number\u201d of files is, nor how many of these \u201cother users\u201d were impacted.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe did not immediately respond to a request for comment, which included questions on those numbers.<\/p>\n<p>In early October, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/23andme-resets-user-passwords-after-genetic-data-posted-online\/\">23andMe disclosed an incident<\/a> in which hackers had stolen some users\u2019 data using a common technique known as \u201ccredential stuffing,\u201d whereby cybercriminals hack into a victim&#8217;s account by using a known password, perhaps leaked due to a data breach on another service.<\/p>\n<p>The damage, however, did not stop with the customers who had their accounts accessed. 23andMe allows users to opt into a feature called <a href=\"https:\/\/customercare.23andme.com\/hc\/en-us\/articles\/212170838\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">DNA Relatives<\/a>. If a user opts-in to that feature, 23andMe shares some of that user\u2019s information with others. That means that by accessing one victim\u2019s account, hackers were also able to see the personal data of people connected to that initial victim.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe said in the filing that for the initial 14,000 users, the stolen data \u201cgenerally included ancestry information, and, for a subset of those accounts, health-related information based upon the user\u2019s genetics.\u201d For the other subset of users, 23andMe only said that the hackers stole \u201cprofile information\u201d and then posted unspecified \u201ccertain information\u201d online.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch analyzed the published sets of stolen data by comparing it to known public genealogy records, including websites published by hobbyists and genealogists. Although the sets of data were formatted differently, they contained some of the same unique user and genetic information that matched genealogy records published online years earlier.<\/p>\n<p>The owner of one genealogy website, for which some of their relatives&#8217; information was exposed in 23andMe&#8217;s data breach, told TechCrunch that they have about 5,000 relatives discovered through 23andMe, and said our &#8220;correlations might take that into account.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>News of the data breach <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wired.com\/story\/23andme-credential-stuffing-data-stolen\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">surfaced online<\/a> in October when hackers advertised the alleged data of one million users of Jewish Ashkenazi descent and 100,000 Chinese users on a well-known hacking forum. Roughly two weeks later, the same hacker who advertised the initial stolen user data <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/hacker-leaks-millions-more-23andme-user-records-on-cybercrime-forum\/\">advertised the alleged records of four million more people<\/a>. The hacker was trying to sell the data of individual victims for $1 to $10.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch found that another hacker on a different hacking forum <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/hackers-advertised-23andme-stolen-data-two-months-ago\/\">had advertised even more allegedly stolen user data two months before<\/a> the advertisement that was initially reported by news outlets in October. In that first advertisement, the hacker claimed to have 300 terabytes of stolen 23andMe user data, and asked for $50 million to sell the whole database, or between $1,000 and $10,000 for a subset of the data.<\/p>\n<p>In response to the data breach, on October 10, 23andMe forced users to reset and change their passwords and encouraged them to turn on multi-factor authentication. And on November 6, the company required all users to use two-step verification, according to the new filing.<\/p>\n<p>After the 23andMe breach, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/07\/23andme-ancestry-myheritage-two-factor-by-default\/\">other DNA testing companies Ancestry and MyHeritage<\/a> started mandating two-factor authentication.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach. In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574594,"featured_media":2636848,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"91ac2367-81de-3b8b-add7-b5d9e6c8c36d","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:43:10Z","apple_news_api_id":"c5bd1269-fbf2-4268-b94f-73a1b33f0581","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:19:29Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/Axb0SafvyQmi5T3Ohsz8FgQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[21587494],"tags":[1156876,51751,965824,67372,61188,1816,190980,78192],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>23andMe says hackers accessed &#039;significant number&#039; of files about users&#039; ancestry | Page 14 of 39 | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"23andMe revealed new details about its data breach. nch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=78192"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636777,"date":"2023-12-01T14:08:57","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:08:57","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636777"},"modified":"2023-12-01T14:59:13","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:59:13","slug":"openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos\/","title":{"rendered":"OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store delayed to 2024 following leadership chaos"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement.<\/p>\n<p>The news was <a href=\"https:\/\/www.axios.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openai-delays-launch-custom-gpt-store-2024\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">first reported by Axios<\/a>, which obtained the memo sent to users and developers. It read in part: &#8220;<span class=\"commtext c00\">we are now planning to launch the GPT Store early next year<\/span>. While we had expected to release it this month, a few unexpected things have been keeping us busy!&#8221; It also detailed some upcoming improvements to the new feature, like a better configuration interface and debug messages.<\/p>\n<p>I have contacted OpenAI and others for further information and will update if they respond.<\/p>\n<p>The launch of the store this month raised eyebrows when it was officially announced at OpenAI&#8217;s Dev Day conference in November. Though there was a working mockup of the store itself and a few of the fine-tuned models called GPTs available to inspect, there were a lot of unanswered questions.<\/p>\n<p>In a Q&amp;A with briefly dethroned CEO Sam Altman and CTO Mira Murati, I asked what the company&#8217;s plan was for the store as far as charging customers and paying developers. Their answer was, essentially, &#8220;we&#8217;ll see.&#8221;<\/p>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/\">App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/embed\/#?secret=AHbO60Xoz7#?secret=cikVeqjbyu\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<p>Given how loose the launch plan was, and that a full week of work at the least was likely lost to the brief but intense battle of board versus CEO, the delay is not much of a surprise. With winter holidays approaching, the idea of shipping a major product under duress while simultaneously navigating a new board and other priorities was probably a non-starter.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For OpenAI customers, GPTs can still be made and shared with others directly, but they will not be publicly listed or able to take part in any revenue-sharing scheme (if OpenAI decides how to implement one) until the store formally launches.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement. The news was first reported by Axios, which [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":12084691,"featured_media":2625182,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"e46aede9-351f-3d3e-89cb-d33b5a31f17a","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:09:04Z","apple_news_api_id":"f5069910-b2a6-44b6-a0f8-17c2f456c705","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T22:59:06Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/A9QaZELKmRLag-BfC9FbHBQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[577047203],"tags":[577089886,421223872],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=421223872"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636715,"date":"2023-12-01T11:24:45","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:24:45","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636715"},"modified":"2023-12-01T11:41:05","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:41:05","slug":"good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms\/","title":{"rendered":"Good old-fashioned AI remains viable in spite of the rise of LLMs"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Remember a year ago, all the way back <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/on-chatgpts-first-anniversary-its-mobile-apps-have-topped-110m-installs-and-nearly-30m-in-revenue\/\">to last November <\/a>before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? That approach seemed to go out the window with the rise of generalized LLMs, but the fact is generalized models aren\u2019t well suited to every problem, and task-based models are still alive and well in the enterprise.<\/p>\n<p>These task-based models have, up until the rise of LLMs, been the basis for most AI in the enterprise, and they aren\u2019t going away. It\u2019s what Amazon CTO Werner Vogels referred to as \u201cgood old-fashioned AI\u201d in his keynote this week, and in his view, is the kind of AI that is still solving a lot of real-world problems.<\/p>\n<p>Atul Deo, general manager of Amazon Bedrock, the product <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/09\/28\/amazon-launches-its-bedrock-generative-ai-service-in-general-availability\/\">introduced earlier this year<\/a> as a way to plug into a variety of large language models via APIs, also believes that task models aren\u2019t going to simply disappear. Instead, they have become another AI tool in the arsenal.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cBefore the advent of large language models, we were mostly in a task-specific world. And the idea there was you would train a model from scratch for a particular task,\u201d Deo told TechCrunch. He says the main difference between the task model and the LLM is that one is trained for that specific task, while the other can handle things outside the boundaries of the model.<\/p>\n<p>Jon Turow, a partner at investment firm Madrona, who formerly spent almost a decade at AWS, says the industry has been talking about emerging capabilities in large language models like reasoning and out-of-domain robustness. \u201cThese allow you to be able to stretch beyond a narrow definition of what the model was initially expected to do,\u201d he said. But, he added, it\u2019s still very much up for debate how far these capabilities can go.<\/p>\n<p>Like Deo, Turow says task models aren\u2019t simply going to suddenly go away. \u201cThere is clearly still a role for task-specific models because they can be smaller, they can be faster, they can be cheaper and they can in some cases even be more performant because they&#8217;re designed for a specific task,\u201d he said.<\/p>\n<p>But the lure of an all-purpose model is hard to ignore. \u201cWhen you&#8217;re looking at an aggregate level in a company, when there are hundreds of machine learning models being trained separately, that doesn&#8217;t make any sense,\u201d Deo said. \u201cWhereas if you went with a more capable large language model, you get the reusability benefit right away, while allowing you to use a single model to tackle a bunch of different use cases.&#8221;<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For Amazon, SageMaker, the company&#8217;s machine learning operations platform, remains a key product, one that is aimed at data scientists instead of developers, as Bedrock is.<a href=\"https:\/\/aws.amazon.com\/blogs\/machine-learning\/aws-celebrates-5-years-of-innovation-with-amazon-sagemaker\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">\u00a0It reports<\/a> tens of thousands of customers building millions of models. It would be foolhardy to give that up, and frankly just because LLMs are the flavor of the moment doesn\u2019t mean that the technology that came before won&#8217;t remain relevant for some time to come.<\/p>\n<p>Enterprise software in particular doesn\u2019t work that way. Nobody is simply tossing their significant investment because a new thing came along, even one as powerful as the current crop of large language models. It&#8217;s worth noting that Amazon did <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-sagemaker-hyperpod-makes-it-easier-to-train-and-fine-tune-llms\/\">announce upgrades to SageMaker<\/a> this week, aimed squarely at managing large language models.<\/p>\n<p>Prior to these more capable large language models, the task model was really the only option, and that\u2019s how companies approached it, by building a team of data scientists to help develop these models. What is the role of the data scientist in the age of large language models where tools are being aimed at developers? Turow thinks they still have a key job to do, even in companies concentrating on LLMs.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cThey&#8217;re going to think critically about data, and that is actually a role that is growing, not shrinking,\u201d he said. Regardless of the model, Turow believes data scientists will help people understand the relationship between AI and data inside large companies.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cI think every one of us needs to really think critically about what AI is and is not capable of and what data does and does not mean,\u201d he said. And that\u2019s true regardless of whether you\u2019re building a more generalized large language model or a task model.<\/p>\n<p>That&#8217;s why these two approaches will continue to work concurrently for some time to come because sometimes bigger is better, and sometimes it\u2019s not.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/aws-reinvent-2023\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-reinvent-2023-banner.jpg\" alt=\"Read more about AWS re:Invent 2023 on TechCrunch\" \/><\/a><\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Remember a year ago, all the way back to last November before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? That approach seemed to go out the window with the rise of generalized LLMs, but the fact is generalized [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":521068,"featured_media":2636724,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"d6eab132-8fbb-3340-9c5e-bc57908e5f9f","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T19:24:52Z","apple_news_api_id":"b119ef43-1e13-4a16-afb0-5d1b45963ffb","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T19:40:54Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAQ==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/AsRnvQx4TShavsF0bRZY_-w","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[577047203,449557044],"tags":[577210801,576717904,577006963],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=577006963"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=577006963"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=577006963"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=577006963"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636577,"date":"2023-12-01T07:50:51","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T15:50:51","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636577"},"modified":"2023-12-01T08:31:21","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T16:31:21","slug":"the-end-of-elon","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/the-end-of-elon\/","title":{"rendered":"The end of Elon"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically over budget and far beyond its original shipping timeline. Plus, just a couple days earlier Musk had essentially sealed the dismal fate of another of his companies by<a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/elon-musk-says-go-fuck-yourself-to-advertisers-leaving-x\/\"> publicly telling advertising partners to &#8220;go fuck [themselves].&#8221;<\/a><\/p>\n<p>To say he was looking diminished is putting it mildly.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck launch, like most of Tesla&#8217;s delivery events, was not a substantive affair. My more patient colleagues have done a much better job actually <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup\/\">recounting what happened there than I ever could<\/a>. Suffice it to say that Musk touted his gaudy stainless steel monstrosity&#8217;s abilities for a while before explaining to a handful of the sycophantic first new Cybertruck owners (including one Alexis Ohanian) the extremely non-obvious way to open their doors and then repeating a vague and perplexing statement about how wild it&#8217;s going to be to see these driving around before calling it a day.<\/p>\n<p>I mentioned the odd choice to stand on the truck bed in semi-darkness despite having a stage lighting setup very clearly designed for him not to do that, but I neglected to note the moment when a sheepish Franz von Holzhausen was drug out to weakly toss a baseball at the Cybertruck&#8217;s windows, in a moment that seemed like unintended satire of when he <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\">smashed the windows of the original prototype at a reveal event in 2019<\/a>. The only takeaway from this watered down reenactment was that if von Holzhausen had replicated the 2019 mishap faithfully, with steel balls and a proper toss, I&#8217;m pretty much convinced he would&#8217;ve smashed the windows again.<\/p>\n<p>Musk didn&#8217;t address his crumbling social media product or how his frequent, inflammatory comments and amplification of false and dangerous conspiracy theories have made it impossible to see a path forward for X that doesn&#8217;t end badly. He didn&#8217;t talk about how his range of distractions, including a repeatedly exploding spacecraft he&#8217;s periodically launching from south Texas, are giving Tesla investors some serious misgivings. Mostly, he talked about the Cybertruck&#8217;s potential benefits to owners in case of the end of modern civilization. And again: about how cool they&#8217;ll look.<\/p>\n<p>No longer the confident, if controversial, prognosticator and big swing solutions engineer, Musk has instead become a mad-eyed, rambling and raving advocate of far-right nonsense and vacillating convictions. Even his seemingly existential objection to OpenAI&#8217;s fast-paced development of AI ended up being little more than petty jealousy, as evidenced by the harried introduction of Grok, his own, far more puerile and muddy-minded equivalent.<\/p>\n<p>Undoubtedly, Musk continues to benefit from legions of devoted fanboys &#8212; many of whom I&#8217;m sure will be letting me know how amazing he still is and how garbage I still am &#8212; but it&#8217;s hard to deny any longer that his influence is on the wane, and the crowning moment of his stupidest idea made flesh in the form of the Cybertruck seems like a meaningful bookend on his era of power.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>&nbsp;<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":5302483,"featured_media":2636578,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"7552398d-df8c-3fa2-92c3-e57ab2c9aeca","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"","apple_news_api_id":"","apple_news_api_modified_at":"","apple_news_api_revision":"","apple_news_api_share_url":"","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,1244446,429989,576716026],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=576716026"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636538,"date":"2023-12-01T09:30:56","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T17:30:56","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636538"},"modified":"2023-12-01T10:12:42","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T18:12:42","slug":"rover-spac-sale-price","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/rover-spac-sale-price\/","title":{"rendered":"Why Rover&#8217;s $2.3B sale price makes good sense"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><span class=\"featured__span-first-words\">Rover is going<\/span> private in a $2.3 billion, all-cash sale to Blackstone, the company announced earlier this week. The pet care\u2013focused company raised <a href=\"https:\/\/www.crunchbase.com\/organization\/rover-com\/company_financials\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">hundreds of millions of dollars<\/a> while private, through a Series G, and later <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2021\/02\/16\/inside-rover-and-moneylions-spac-led-public-debuts\/\">went public via a SPAC<\/a>. Notably, unlike a great many SPAC combinations, Rover is proving that blank-check companies are not merely a way to incinerate wealth.<\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p style=\"text-align: center;\"><strong><em>The Exchange explores startups, markets and money. <\/em><\/strong><\/p>\n<p style=\"text-align: center;\"><strong><em>Read it <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/subscribe\/?tpcc=theexchange\">every morning on TechCrunch+<\/a> or get <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/newsletters\/?utm_source=internal&amp;utm_medium=WPunit\">The Exchange newsletter<\/a> every Saturday.<\/em><\/strong><\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p>The former startup has a 30-day shopping provision built into its deal with Blackstone, meaning that other offers may come to the fore. However, with the private equity group paying a stiff premium for Rover shares \u2014 61% more than the company&#8217;s 90-day volume weighted average share price, <a href=\"https:\/\/www.blackstone.com\/news\/press\/rover-agrees-to-be-acquired-by-blackstone-in-2-3-billion-transaction\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">per a release<\/a> \u2014 that doesn&#8217;t sound too likely.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/the-exchange\/\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" class=\"alignright wp-image-2220369 size-medium\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?w=300\" alt=\"\" width=\"300\" height=\"300\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg 900w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=150,150 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=300,300 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=768,768 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=680,680 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=32,32 32w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=50,50 50w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=64,64 64w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=96,96 96w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=128,128 128w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px\" \/><\/a>The Rover deal is expensive but it has some notable caveats that tell us quite a lot about the state of the market for tech, and tech-enabled, companies. I&#8217;ll bet you didn&#8217;t expect a pet-focused e-commerce marketplace to earn an 8.7x revenue run rate multiple in 2023!<\/p>\n<p>This morning I want to dig into why I think Blackstone is paying so much for Rover, what we can glean from that research for other startups, and why a select few SPAC&#8217;d public companies that are trading like literal dogs may be bargains for the right buyer.<\/p>\n<h2>That price tag isn&#8217;t unreasonable<\/h2>\n<p>Rover and Blackstone announced their transaction after we got the former <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.rover.com\/news-releases\/news-release-details\/rover-reports-third-quarter-2023-financial-results\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">company&#8217;s Q3 2023 results<\/a>, meaning that we have pretty up-to-date figures concerning its recent performance.<\/p>\n<p>Rover reported third-quarter revenues of $66.2 million, up 30% from the same period one year ago. The company also flipped to GAAP net income and told investors that it intended to buy back more of its own shares. It also <a href=\"https:\/\/www.investorsobserver.com\/news\/stock-update\/rover-group-inc-up-23-65-to-8-00-after-earnings-beat\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">beat guidance<\/a> in the quarter.<div class=\"extra-crunch-offer-container\"><\/div>","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>The Rover deal is expensive, but it has some notable caveats that tell us quite a lot about the state of the market for tech, and tech-enabled, companies.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":428363,"featured_media":2621031,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"2d6e9d10-1a36-38b5-8ae0-2e959cef7c2e","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"","apple_news_api_id":"","apple_news_api_modified_at":"","apple_news_api_revision":"","apple_news_api_share_url":"","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[20429],"tags":[577025428,29172,576765839],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[576796356],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- Link","children":[]}]},"entities":{"posts":[{"id":2636845,"date":"2023-12-01T14:43:04","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:43:04","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636845"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:19:36","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:19:36","slug":"23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry\/","title":{"rendered":"23andMe says hackers accessed &#8216;significant number&#8217; of files about users&#8217; ancestry"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/www.sec.gov\/ix?doc=\/Archives\/edgar\/data\/1804591\/000119312523287449\/d242666d8ka.htm\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission<\/a> published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer base. <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.23andme.com\/news-releases\/news-release-details\/23andme-reports-fy2023-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-financial\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">According to the company&#8217;s most recent annual earnings report<\/a>, 23andMe has \u201cmore than 14 million customers worldwide,\u201d which means 0.1% is around 14,000.<\/p>\n<p>But the company also said that by accessing those accounts, the hackers were also able to access \u201ca significant number of files containing profile information about other users\u2019 ancestry that such users chose to share when opting in to 23andMe\u2019s DNA Relatives feature.\u201d<\/p>\n<p>The company did not specify what that \u201csignificant number\u201d of files is, nor how many of these \u201cother users\u201d were impacted.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe did not immediately respond to a request for comment, which included questions on those numbers.<\/p>\n<p>In early October, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/23andme-resets-user-passwords-after-genetic-data-posted-online\/\">23andMe disclosed an incident<\/a> in which hackers had stolen some users\u2019 data using a common technique known as \u201ccredential stuffing,\u201d whereby cybercriminals hack into a victim&#8217;s account by using a known password, perhaps leaked due to a data breach on another service.<\/p>\n<p>The damage, however, did not stop with the customers who had their accounts accessed. 23andMe allows users to opt into a feature called <a href=\"https:\/\/customercare.23andme.com\/hc\/en-us\/articles\/212170838\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">DNA Relatives<\/a>. If a user opts-in to that feature, 23andMe shares some of that user\u2019s information with others. That means that by accessing one victim\u2019s account, hackers were also able to see the personal data of people connected to that initial victim.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe said in the filing that for the initial 14,000 users, the stolen data \u201cgenerally included ancestry information, and, for a subset of those accounts, health-related information based upon the user\u2019s genetics.\u201d For the other subset of users, 23andMe only said that the hackers stole \u201cprofile information\u201d and then posted unspecified \u201ccertain information\u201d online.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch analyzed the published sets of stolen data by comparing it to known public genealogy records, including websites published by hobbyists and genealogists. Although the sets of data were formatted differently, they contained some of the same unique user and genetic information that matched genealogy records published online years earlier.<\/p>\n<p>The owner of one genealogy website, for which some of their relatives&#8217; information was exposed in 23andMe&#8217;s data breach, told TechCrunch that they have about 5,000 relatives discovered through 23andMe, and said our &#8220;correlations might take that into account.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>News of the data breach <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wired.com\/story\/23andme-credential-stuffing-data-stolen\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">surfaced online<\/a> in October when hackers advertised the alleged data of one million users of Jewish Ashkenazi descent and 100,000 Chinese users on a well-known hacking forum. Roughly two weeks later, the same hacker who advertised the initial stolen user data <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/hacker-leaks-millions-more-23andme-user-records-on-cybercrime-forum\/\">advertised the alleged records of four million more people<\/a>. The hacker was trying to sell the data of individual victims for $1 to $10.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch found that another hacker on a different hacking forum <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/hackers-advertised-23andme-stolen-data-two-months-ago\/\">had advertised even more allegedly stolen user data two months before<\/a> the advertisement that was initially reported by news outlets in October. In that first advertisement, the hacker claimed to have 300 terabytes of stolen 23andMe user data, and asked for $50 million to sell the whole database, or between $1,000 and $10,000 for a subset of the data.<\/p>\n<p>In response to the data breach, on October 10, 23andMe forced users to reset and change their passwords and encouraged them to turn on multi-factor authentication. And on November 6, the company required all users to use two-step verification, according to the new filing.<\/p>\n<p>After the 23andMe breach, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/07\/23andme-ancestry-myheritage-two-factor-by-default\/\">other DNA testing companies Ancestry and MyHeritage<\/a> started mandating two-factor authentication.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach. In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574594,"featured_media":2636848,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"91ac2367-81de-3b8b-add7-b5d9e6c8c36d","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:43:10Z","apple_news_api_id":"c5bd1269-fbf2-4268-b94f-73a1b33f0581","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:19:29Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/Axb0SafvyQmi5T3Ohsz8FgQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[21587494],"tags":[1156876,51751,965824,67372,61188,1816,190980,78192],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>23andMe says hackers accessed &#039;significant number&#039; of files about users&#039; ancestry | Page 14 of 39 | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"23andMe revealed new details about its data breach. nch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=78192"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636827,"date":"2023-12-01T14:25:05","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:25:05","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636827"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:09:06","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:09:06","slug":"ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time\/","title":{"rendered":"Ev startup Fisker cut its 2023 production target for the fourth time"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Fisker, the California-based EV startup, cut its annual production guidance in an effort to free up $300 million in working capital, the company said in a business update Friday.<\/p>\n<p>Fisker said it expects to produce about 10,000 vehicles this year. The decision comes less than a month since Fisker cut its production target to between 13,000 and 17,000 vehicles for 2023. The production guidance is just a quarter of Fisker&#8217;s bullish forecast from a year ago. In November 2022, Fisker said it planned to produce <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/02\/fisker-bumps-up-production-for-all-electric-ocean-suv\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">42,400 Ocean SUVs<\/a> by the end of 2023 due to strong demand in the U.S. and Europe. That rosy projection was slashed in May to 32,000-36,000 vehicles and then cut again in August to 20,000-23,000 vehicles. This latest update makes four reductions since the spring.<\/p>\n<p>The production cut will allow the company to access $300 million in working capital, giving the company &#8220;flexibility,&#8221; according to the business update.<\/p>\n<p>&#8220;Our teams have worked hard to overcome some early delivery challenges and are now setting an impressive pace as we prepare to close out 2023,&#8221; Chairman and CEO Henrik Fisker said in a statement. &#8220;We may not have hit our original forecast but taking current market conditions and negative sentiments around EV sales into account, I would say we are doing quite well, as we continue to accelerate sales and deliveries. This is yielding considerable revenue as we ramp up our business. I expect by the end of this year we will have delivered more customer cars than any Western EV startup did in their first year of deliveries. The company continues to sharpen its focus on growing its current markets and enhancing our sales and service offerings for the Fisker Ocean.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>Fisker said <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.fiskerinc.com\/news\/news-details\/2023\/Fisker-Provides-Business-Update\/default.aspx\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">in its business update<\/a> that it has also launched a new strategy to improve deliveries in the U.S. and Europe, which helped it overcome early logistics hurdles. While Fisker didn&#8217;t elaborate on exactly what those challenges were, it appears the strategy involves adding more transportation logistics companies to speed up deliveries, increased outreach to reservation holders and opening more facilities dedicated to retail, deliveries and service.<\/p>\n<p>The company said it&#8217;s also launching a leasing program in the U.S., Canada and Europe, but didn&#8217;t include details on when that might occur.<\/p>\n<p>Fisker also provided an update around hiring, importantly Dan Quirk as its new executive vice president of finance and accounting. The hiring comes after Fisker lost two chief accounting officers in short succession and delayed the filing of its quarterly earnings report with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Other hires include Axel Buhr as vice president of finance and controller operations, Ram Iyer as senior VP of EE integration and validation and Wolfgang Hoffmann as country manager in Canada, where Fisker is about to begin deliveries.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Fisker, the California-based EV startup, cut its annual production guidance in an effort to free up $300 million in working capital, the company said in a business update Friday. Fisker said it expects to produce about 10,000 vehicles this year. The decision comes less than a month since Fisker cut its production target to between [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574188,"featured_media":2566477,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"8ab52a9f-f276-3d6d-99b5-b3d7248867f4","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:25:10Z","apple_news_api_id":"0f379758-3879-440c-9165-d92cdf4c9e3c","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:09:00Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/ADzeXWDh5RAyRZdks30yePA","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[20429,2401],"tags":[197443,76801,495498],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Ev startup Fisker cut its 2023 production target for the fourth time | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"California EV startup Fisker has cut its production guidance again for 2023. runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=495498"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636777,"date":"2023-12-01T14:08:57","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:08:57","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636777"},"modified":"2023-12-01T14:59:13","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:59:13","slug":"openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos\/","title":{"rendered":"OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store delayed to 2024 following leadership chaos"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement.<\/p>\n<p>The news was <a href=\"https:\/\/www.axios.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openai-delays-launch-custom-gpt-store-2024\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">first reported by Axios<\/a>, which obtained the memo sent to users and developers. It read in part: &#8220;<span class=\"commtext c00\">we are now planning to launch the GPT Store early next year<\/span>. While we had expected to release it this month, a few unexpected things have been keeping us busy!&#8221; It also detailed some upcoming improvements to the new feature, like a better configuration interface and debug messages.<\/p>\n<p>I have contacted OpenAI and others for further information and will update if they respond.<\/p>\n<p>The launch of the store this month raised eyebrows when it was officially announced at OpenAI&#8217;s Dev Day conference in November. Though there was a working mockup of the store itself and a few of the fine-tuned models called GPTs available to inspect, there were a lot of unanswered questions.<\/p>\n<p>In a Q&amp;A with briefly dethroned CEO Sam Altman and CTO Mira Murati, I asked what the company&#8217;s plan was for the store as far as charging customers and paying developers. Their answer was, essentially, &#8220;we&#8217;ll see.&#8221;<\/p>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/\">App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/embed\/#?secret=AHbO60Xoz7#?secret=cikVeqjbyu\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<p>Given how loose the launch plan was, and that a full week of work at the least was likely lost to the brief but intense battle of board versus CEO, the delay is not much of a surprise. With winter holidays approaching, the idea of shipping a major product under duress while simultaneously navigating a new board and other priorities was probably a non-starter.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For OpenAI customers, GPTs can still be made and shared with others directly, but they will not be publicly listed or able to take part in any revenue-sharing scheme (if OpenAI decides how to implement one) until the store formally launches.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement. The news was first reported by Axios, which /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=421223872"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2634605,"date":"2023-12-01T13:30:07","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T21:30:07","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2634605"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:56:59","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:56:59","slug":"heres-everything-aws-reinvent-2023-so-far","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/heres-everything-aws-reinvent-2023-so-far\/","title":{"rendered":"AWS re:Invent: Everything Amazon&#8217;s announced, from new AI tools to LLM updates and more"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><span class=\"featured__span-first-words\">Amazon Web Services<\/span> took to Las Vegas for its AWS re:Invent event, which kicked off November 27 and runs until December 1. Amazon delivered a rapid-fire series of announcements and unveilings of recent things it&#8217;s been working on, in a direct response to the increased competition for cloud providers to meet the rise of AI with compelling offers for customers. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-takes-the-cheap-shots\/\">AWS CEO Adam Selipsky&#8217;s keynote<\/a> set the tone for the event, making it clear that AWS is in a position to defend its longstanding lead, and it&#8217;s deploying AI tools and services to continue being the top large cloud provider in the market.<\/p>\n<p>We know you might not have time to attend<span style=\"font-weight: 400;\">, so we\u2019re taking that on for the next few days and will deliver quick hits of the biggest news as they are announced, all in an easy-to-digest, easy-to-skim list featuring on-the-ground reporting from editors and cloud experts <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/author\/frederic-lardinois\/\">Frederic Lardinois<\/a> and <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/author\/ronjourn\/\">Ron Miller<\/a>, along with contributions from the wider TechCrunch team. Here we go!<\/span><\/p>\n<h2>Friday, Dec. 1<\/h2>\n<h2>One Last Bit of AWS Analysis<\/h2>\n<p>AWS re:Invent is coming to a close, but the implications of the AI announcements and Amazon&#8217;s approach to this year&#8217;s conference remain with us. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/amazon-ai-catchup-microsoft\/\">Ron put a pin on this year&#8217;s event with a TC+ exclusive piece<\/a> that makes it clear Amazon was setting its sights on one major company in particular with its event this year: Microsoft. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/amazon-ai-catchup-microsoft\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Thursday, Nov. 30<\/h2>\n<h2>Q&amp;A with Amazon CTO Werner Vogels<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2635784\" style=\"width: 690px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2635784\" class=\"wp-image-2635784 size-large\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?w=680\" alt=\"\" width=\"680\" height=\"453\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg 2400w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=150,100 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=300,200 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=768,512 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=680,453 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=1536,1024 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=2048,1365 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=1200,800 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-1518931881.jpg?resize=50,33 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 680px) 100vw, 680px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2635784\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Credits:<\/strong> Johannes Simon\/Getty Images<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>Ahead of <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/amazon-cto-werner-vogels-on-culturally-aware-llms-developer-productivity-and-femtech\/\">Amazon CTO Werner Vogels\u2019<\/a>\u00a0keynote address, Frederic sat down with Vogels to discuss the trends he is seeing \u2014 generative AI, of course \u2014 and predictions for the coming year. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/amazon-cto-werner-vogels-on-culturally-aware-llms-developer-productivity-and-femtech\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Wednesday, Nov. 29<\/h2>\n<h2>Neptune Analytics<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2635424\" style=\"width: 690px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/with-neptune-analytics-aws-combines-the-power-of-vector-search-and-graph-data\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2635424\" class=\"wp-image-2635424 size-large\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?w=680\" alt=\"Amazon Neptune Analytics' reveal from the AWS reinvent conference in 2023\" width=\"680\" height=\"380\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png 2222w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=300,168 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=768,429 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=680,380 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=1536,859 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=2048,1145 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=1200,671 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.35.50-AM.png?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 680px) 100vw, 680px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2635424\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> AWS<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>Another new tool is <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/with-neptune-analytics-aws-combines-the-power-of-vector-search-and-graph-data\/\">Amazon Neptune Analytics<\/a>, which combines the best of both worlds: graph and vector databases. Ron reports that the new service helps customers analyze existing Neptune graph data or data lakes on top of S3 storage, taking advantage of vector search to find key insights. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/with-neptune-analytics-aws-combines-the-power-of-vector-search-and-graph-data\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<h2>Clean Rooms ML<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2635505\" style=\"width: 690px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2635505\" class=\"size-large wp-image-2635505\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?w=680\" alt=\"AWS Clean Rooms ML helps you to unlock various opportunities for you to generate insights.\" width=\"680\" height=\"353\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png 1920w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=150,78 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=300,156 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=768,398 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=680,353 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=1536,797 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=1200,623 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-cleanrooms-ml-rev-0.png?resize=50,26 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 680px) 100vw, 680px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2635505\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">AWS Clean Rooms ML helps you to unlock various opportunities for you to generate insights.<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Amazon\u2019s launching a privacy-preserving service that lets AWS customers deploy \u201clookalike\u201d AI models trained for one-off company-company collaborations called <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/aws-clean-rooms-ml-lets-companies-securely-collaborate-on-ai\/\">AWS Clean Rooms ML<\/a>, which Kyle writes is an offshoot of AWS\u2019 existing <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/29\/aws-gets-data-clean-rooms-for-analytics-data\/\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/29\/aws-gets-data-clean-rooms-for-analytics-data\/\">Clean Rooms<\/a> product and removes the need for AWS customers to share proprietary data with their outside partners to build, train and deploy AI models. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/aws-clean-rooms-ml-lets-companies-securely-collaborate-on-ai\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>SageMaker HyperPod<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2635405\" style=\"width: 690px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2635405\" class=\"wp-image-2635405 size-large\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?w=680\" alt=\"AWS SageMaker Hyperpod's reveal from the AWS reinvent conference in 2023\" width=\"680\" height=\"380\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png 2222w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=300,168 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=768,429 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=680,380 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=1536,859 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=2048,1145 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=1200,671 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-9.20.50-AM.png?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 680px) 100vw, 680px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2635405\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> AWS<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>From Amazon\u2019s AWS cloud arm comes the <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-sagemaker-hyperpod-makes-it-easier-to-train-and-fine-tune-llms\/\">Amazon SageMaker HyperPod<\/a>, a new purpose-built service for training and fine-tuning large language models. Frederic spoke with Ankur Mehrotra, AWS\u2019 general manager for SageMaker, ahead of today&#8217;s announcement, who said, \u201cSageMaker HyperPod gives you the ability to create a distributed cluster with accelerated instances that\u2019s optimized for disputed training. It gives you the tools to efficiently distribute models and data across your cluster \u2014 and that speeds up your training process.\u201d <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-sagemaker-hyperpod-makes-it-easier-to-train-and-fine-tune-llms\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>AWS Titan image generator<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2635374\" style=\"width: 690px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-finally-releases-its-own-ai-powered-image-generator\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2635374\" class=\"wp-image-2635374 size-large\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?w=680\" alt=\"AWS Titan Image Generator revealed on stage at AWS reinvent 2023\" width=\"680\" height=\"380\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png 2222w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=300,168 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=768,429 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=680,380 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=1536,859 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=2048,1145 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=1200,671 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-29-at-8.52.52-AM.png?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 680px) 100vw, 680px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2635374\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> AWS<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>You read that right, Amazon is joining the ranks of other big techs in finally releasing its own <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-finally-releases-its-own-ai-powered-image-generator\/\">image generator<\/a>. Kyle reports that the Titan Image Generator is now available in preview for AWS customers and can create new images when given a text description or customize existing images. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-finally-releases-its-own-ai-powered-image-generator\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Tuesday, Nov. 28<\/h2>\n<h2>Amazon Q: An AI-powered chatbot<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2634761\" style=\"width: 3254px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/amazon-unveils-q-an-ai-powered-chatbot-for-businesses\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2634761\" class=\"breakout wp-image-2634761 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png\" alt=\"Amazon Q, an AI-powered chatbot, revealed on-stage at AWS Reinvent 2023\" width=\"3244\" height=\"1270\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png 3244w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=150,59 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=300,117 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=768,301 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=680,266 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=1536,601 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=2048,802 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=1200,470 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screen-Shot-2023-11-28-at-11.44.40-AM-1.png?resize=50,20 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 3244px) 100vw, 3244px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2634761\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">Amazon Q is Amazon&#8217;s new AI-powered chatbot. <strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Amazon<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>The big announcement for the day was <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/amazon-unveils-q-an-ai-powered-chatbot-for-businesses\/\">Amazon Q, an AI-powered chatbot for AWS customers<\/a>. During his keynote, AWS CEO Adam Selipsky described it as being able to &#8220;easily chat, generate content and take actions. It\u2019s all informed by an understanding of your systems, your data repositories and your operations.\u201d Kyle reports that Q is trained on 17 years\u2019 worth of AWS knowledge and will go beyond just answering questions \u2014 it will also do things like understand the nuances of app workloads and suggest AWS solutions and products for apps that only run for a few seconds. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/amazon-unveils-q-an-ai-powered-chatbot-for-businesses\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2634742\" style=\"width: 1747px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-adds-guardrails-for-amazon-bedrock-to-help-safeguard-llms\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2634742\" class=\"breakout wp-image-2634742 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-amazon-bedrock-guardrails-01.png\" alt=\"AWS Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock\"
