https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-amazon-bedrock-guardrails-01.png?resize=1200,772 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/2023-amazon-bedrock-guardrails-01.png?resize=50,32 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1737px) 100vw, 1737px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2634742\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">AWS Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock. <strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Amazon<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>The new Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock tool lets companies define and limit the kinds of language a model can use. For example, define topics that are out of bounds for the model, so it simply doesn\u2019t answer irrelevant questions, Ron writes. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-adds-guardrails-for-amazon-bedrock-to-help-safeguard-llms\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>New AWS Trainium chips for AI models<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2634677\" style=\"width: 490px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2634677\" class=\"wp-image-2634677 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/AWS_Graviton4_and_AWS_Trainium2_prototype.jpg\" alt=\"AWS Graviton4\" width=\"480\" height=\"199\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/AWS_Graviton4_and_AWS_Trainium2_prototype.jpg 480w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/AWS_Graviton4_and_AWS_Trainium2_prototype.jpg?resize=150,62 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/AWS_Graviton4_and_AWS_Trainium2_prototype.jpg?resize=300,124 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/AWS_Graviton4_and_AWS_Trainium2_prototype.jpg?resize=50,21 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 480px) 100vw, 480px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2634677\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">AWS Graviton4 and AWS Trainium (prototype). <strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Business Wire<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/amazon-unveils-new-chips-for-training-and-running-ai-models\/\">Amazon unveiled the latest generation of its chips for model training and inferencing<\/a> (i.e. running trained models). Kyle writes that Amazon already discussed AWS Trainium2, designed to deliver up to 4x better performance and 2x better energy efficiency than the first-generation Trainium. The second chip was announced this morning, called the Graviton4, is intended for inferencing. The fourth generation in Amazon\u2019s Graviton chip family (as implied by the \u201c4\u201d appended to \u201cGraviton\u201d), it\u2019s distinct from Amazon\u2019s other inferencing chip, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/29\/amazon-announces-preview-of-new-inf2-instances-designed-for-larger-models\/\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/29\/amazon-announces-preview-of-new-inf2-instances-designed-for-larger-models\/\">Inferentia<\/a>. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/amazon-unveils-new-chips-for-training-and-running-ai-models\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Amazon S3 Express One Zone<\/h2>\n<p>Amazon has a major update to its S3 object storage service called <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-launches-s3-express-one-zone-promises-10x-write-speed-improvement\/\">Amazon S3 Express One Zone<\/a>, a new high-performance and low latency tier for S3. Frederic reports that One Zone will offer a major performance improvement for data-intensive applications, including AI\/ML training, financial modeling and high-performance computing. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-launches-s3-express-one-zone-promises-10x-write-speed-improvement\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Three new serverless offerings<\/h2>\n<p>Amazon announced <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/amazon-announces-three-new-serverless-offerings-to-kick-off-reinvent\/\">three new serverless offerings<\/a> to make it easier to manage Aurora, ElastiCache and Redshift serverless services. Ron writes, &#8220;Because each of these options is serverless, it means that Amazon manages all of the hardware in the background, and delivers just the right amount of resources you need, scaling up when needed without IT having to deal with all of the back-end management work.&#8221; <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/amazon-announces-three-new-serverless-offerings-to-kick-off-reinvent\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Amazon One palm-scanning expands from stores to security<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2634443\" style=\"width: 1930px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2634443\" class=\"breakout wp-image-2634443 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg\" alt=\"Amazon One Enterprise\" width=\"1920\" height=\"1078\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg 1920w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=300,168 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=768,431 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=680,382 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=1536,862 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=1200,674 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Amazon-One-Enterprise_AOE_Office-Entry_hand-hover_alt-person-4-e1701169559480.jpg?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1920px) 100vw, 1920px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2634443\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">Amazon One Enterprise. <strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Amazon<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>AWS lifted the lid on a new palm-scanning identity service that allows companies to authenticate people when entering physical premises. Paul reports that Amazon One Enterprise builds on the company\u2019s existing Amazon One offering, which it <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2020\/09\/29\/amazon-introduces-the-amazon-one-a-way-to-pay-with-your-palm-when-entering-stores\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2020\/09\/29\/amazon-introduces-the-amazon-one-a-way-to-pay-with-your-palm-when-entering-stores\/\">debuted back in 2020<\/a>\u00a0to enable biometric payments in\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2018\/01\/21\/inside-amazons-surveillance-powered-no-checkout-convenience-store\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2018\/01\/21\/inside-amazons-surveillance-powered-no-checkout-convenience-store\/\">Amazon\u2019s own surveillance-powered cashierless stores<\/a>. Visitors to Amazon Go stores can associate their payment card with their palm print, allowing them to enter the store and complete their transaction by hovering their hand over a scanner. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-brings-amazon-one-palm-scanning-authentication-technology-to-the-enterprise\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>New thin client virtual desktop environment devices<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2633830\" style=\"width: 2650px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2633830\" class=\"breakout wp-image-2633830 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg\" alt=\"AWS thin client on table with two monitors\" width=\"2640\" height=\"1486\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg 2640w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=300,169 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=768,432 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=680,383 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=1536,865 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=2048,1153 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=1200,675 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-thin-client.jpg?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 2640px) 100vw, 2640px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2633830\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Amazon<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/amazons-new-195-thin-client-looks-just-like-a-fire-tv-cube\/\">Amazon launched new $195 devices<\/a> that allow enterprise users to access virtual desktop environments, like Amazon WorkSpaces, over the internet. Sarah writes that the devices are housed in Fire TV Cube hardware \u2014 a decision Amazon made to leverage existing expertise from the arm of the retail giant that makes streaming media players. The company explained its decision to build new hardware came from customer feedback about wanting to lower IT spending by replacing desktops and laptops with less expensive hardware. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/amazons-new-195-thin-client-looks-just-like-a-fire-tv-cube\/\">Read more<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/aws-reinvent-2023\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-reinvent-2023-banner.jpg\" alt=\"Read more about AWS re:Invent 2023 on TechCrunch\" \/><\/a><\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>We will deliver quick hits of the biggest news from AWS re:Invent as they are announced.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574474,"featured_media":2634610,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"79d55f15-0ce9-3914-974f-81a93854e716","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-11-28T16:23:12Z","apple_news_api_id":"348eb6f4-8d7e-4a4f-8a5b-ba4d681cbee2","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:57:08Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAFg==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/ANI629I1-Sk-KW7pNaBy-4g","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[449557044],"tags":[6602,144203,126070658,577210801],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- name=search_term_string"}],"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Organization","@id":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/#organization","name":"TechCrunch","alternateName":"TC","url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/","logo":{"@type":"ImageObject","inLanguage":"en-US","@id":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/#\/schema\/logo\/image\/","url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2018\/04\/tc-logo-2018-square-reverse2x.png?resize=1200,1200","contentUrl":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2018\/04\/tc-logo-2018-square-reverse2x.png?resize=1200,1200","width":1200,"height":1200,"caption":"TechCrunch"},"image":{"@id":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/#\/schema\/logo\/image\/"},"sameAs":["http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/techcrunch","http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/TechCrunch","http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/mstdn.social\/@TechCrunch","http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/bsky.app\/profile\/techcrunch.bsky.social","http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.threads.net\/@techcrunch"]}]}},"_links":{"self":[{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tags\/577210801"}],"collection":[{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tags"}],"about":[{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/taxonomies\/post_tag"}],"wp:post_type":[{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/posts?tags=577210801"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=577210801"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=577210801"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=577210801"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=577210801"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2634689,"date":"2023-12-01T12:10:46","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:10:46","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2634689"},"modified":"2023-12-01T12:19:06","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:19:06","slug":"whats-up-with-teslas-cybertruck-everything-to-know-about-the-much-hyped-electric-pickup","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/whats-up-with-teslas-cybertruck-everything-to-know-about-the-much-hyped-electric-pickup\/","title":{"rendered":"What&#8217;s up with Tesla&#8217;s Cybertruck? Everything to know about the much-hyped electric pickup"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">After four years, the long-awaited launch of the Tesla Cybertruck electric pickup has come and gone.<\/p>\n<p>The boxy vehicle is Tesla\u2019s first new model since 2020, when it <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2020\/03\/17\/model-y-deliveries-begin-heres-what-is-new-in-teslas-ev-crossover\/\">started delivering<\/a> the Model Y. Yet, Cybertruck&#8217;s initial debut predates that moment; Tesla CEO Elon Musk showed off an early version of the pickup at a memorable <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/behold-the-tesla-cybertruck-is-here\/\">2019 event<\/a>, when it accidentally <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\">smashed two windows<\/a> while attempting to demonstrate Cybertruck\u2019s durability.<\/p>\n<p>Here we\u2019ll answer some questions we figured a brave explorer such as yourself might ask (err&#8230; type in a search bar) about Tesla\u2019s Cybertruck, including details on the vehicle\u2019s specs, availability and design of the vehicle. The first deliveries, in which about 10 high-profile customers like Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian took possession of the truck, occurred November 30 at the Tesla Gigafactory in Austin.<\/p>\n<p>While we have lots of details it&#8217;s still unclear if the Cybertruck <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/innovation-or-folly-the-cybertruck-will-test-whether-anyone-still-trusts-tesla\/\">will kick off the vehicle&#8217;s journey toward success, or failure<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>What is the Cybertruck?<\/h2>\n<p>The Cybertruck is a steel-clad electric pickup truck made by Tesla. According to Musk, the vehicle measures fewer than 19 feet long and features a bed longer than six feet. It has four doors and room for six adults.<\/p>\n<p>Tesla shared Cybertruck specs <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/behold-the-tesla-cybertruck-is-here\/\">in 2019<\/a>, promising three variants of the vehicle. That included a $39,900 single-motor version with rear-wheel drive and a 250-mile range. But this was eons ago, figuratively speaking. At least one variant is reported to weigh <a href=\"https:\/\/supercarblondie.com\/cybertrucks-weight-revealed\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">about as much as a Hummer<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p>Tesla is planning to offer three variants of the Cybertruck, but the prices, range and other specs have changed since 2019.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>The cheapest Cybertruck, a single-motor rear-wheel version with 250 miles of range, a 6.9-second zero to 60 miles per hour acceleration rate, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/teslas-cheapest-cybertruck-wont-drop-until-2025\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">won&#8217;t be available until 2025<\/a>. The all-wheel drive variant has an estimated 340-mile range and top speed of 112 mph.<\/p>\n<p>The third variant, called the Cyberbeast, has three motors that delivers 845 horsepower, a 2.6-second 0 to 60 mph acceleration rate and a top speed of 130 mph. The Cyberbeast has an estimated 320-mile range and $99,990 price tag. Both of the all-wheel drive and Cyberbeast versions have a claimed towing capacity of 11,000 pounds.<\/p>\n<p>The company is also going to offer a range extender that will push the all-wheel drive version to an estimated 470 miles and the Cyberbeast to more than 440 miles of range. After the event, Musk took to X, formerly Twitter, to give a few more details about this add on. He said the range extender will be an &#8220;optional pack that fits in about 1\/3 of the truck bed. Still room for plenty of cargo. It&#8217;s meant for very long trips or towing heavy things up mountains.&#8221;<\/p>\n<h2>Why does the Cybertruck look like that?<\/h2>\n<p>Also referred to as Cybrtrk in an early <a href=\"https:\/\/electrek.co\/2019\/11\/14\/tesla-files-for-cybrtrk-trademark-in-advance-of-nov-21-pickup-truck-reveal\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">trademark<\/a>, the Cybertruck\u2019s name, neon logo and exterior evoke a sort of cyberpunk, post-apocalyptic aesthetic. Why? Because Musk wants it to.<\/p>\n<p>The electric pickup is made of steel, a tough material that resists rust but is hard to shape. The material\u2019s rigidity influenced the EV\u2019s starkly geometric design; it also led to <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wsj.com\/business\/autos\/behind-teslas-challenges-in-making-the-cybertruck-ultrahard-stainless-steel-8cb4c1b9\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">launch delays<\/a> and <a href=\"https:\/\/www.thedrive.com\/news\/we-saw-the-tesla-cybertruck-up-close-panel-gaps-and-all\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">panel gaps<\/a> on Cybertruck test vehicles.<\/p>\n<p>Musk has <a href=\"https:\/\/twitter.com\/elonmusk\/status\/1719518654063096087\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">described<\/a> the tank-like vehicle as \u201can armored personnel carrier from the future \u2014 what Bladerunner would have driven.\u201d Crucially, while Bladerunner is a stunning film, it depicts a <a href=\"https:\/\/maxread.substack.com\/p\/lets-clear-up-a-few-things-about\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">horrific dystopia<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p><span class=\"embed-youtube embed--youtube embed embed-jetpack breakout embed--video\" style=\"text-align:center; display: block;\"><iframe class=\"youtube-player embed-iframe embed-iframe--youtube\" width=\"640\" height=\"360\" src=\"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/embed\/DsonSEllPmU?version=3&#038;rel=1&#038;showsearch=0&#038;showinfo=1&#038;iv_load_policy=1&#038;fs=1&#038;hl=en-US&#038;autohide=2&#038;wmode=transparent\" allowfullscreen=\"true\" style=\"border:0;\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-popups allow-presentation allow-popups-to-escape-sandbox\"><\/iframe><\/span><\/p>\n<h2>Is the Cybertruck for sale yet?<\/h2>\n<p>Tesla expects to <a href=\"https:\/\/www.engadget.com\/tesla-cybertruck-mass-production-164029626.html?guccounter=1&amp;guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&amp;guce_referrer_sig=AQAAANJQHtiBtrO6BAIZwDQjXbJ-9Dt8dlZYRXNywfIzSG425S49zCptM9IgYxGhVGLUIswp61JZNAzxH8KftNOzRpaV-fRnbFj3YvbLsymQH7pqmZuzDLvJ9l-GAjmu-dBibWs83r7564CQbbjAyMFMpoy2LL1jSBD77FcAAYtMr5qp\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">mass produce<\/a> Cybertrucks starting in 2024, but unless you\u2019re a wealthy early adopter with special Tesla connections, securing a Cybertruck won\u2019t be easy early on. You\u2019ll need to get in line behind the folks who reserved the truck as far back as 2019.<\/p>\n<p>That said, you may be able to jump ahead for a price: Apparently, <a href=\"https:\/\/www.ebay.com\/itm\/266461806182\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Cybertruck reservations<\/a> have appeared on eBay for $10,000. Proceed with caution. I&#8217;m not sure how easy it&#8217;d be to verify if the offers are legit, and Musk has threatened to punish resellers in the past.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck price tags varies depending on the variant and add ons. The tl;dr is that all of the considerably prices are higher than advertised back in 2019. The rear-wheel version \u2014 which, reminder isn&#8217;t available until 2025 \u2014 starts at $60,990. The all-wheel drive version has a $79,990 starting price and the Cyberbeast comes in just shy of $100,000.<\/p>\n<p>The federal tax credit for EVs has a number of eligibility requirements including price. Under current rules, the trucks and SUVs priced above $80,000 don&#8217;t qualify for the $7,500 credit.<\/p>\n<h2>Can I resell my Cybertruck?<\/h2>\n<p>Tesla indeed <a href=\"https:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231113101113\/https:\/\/www.tesla.com\/configurator\/api\/v3\/terms?locale=en_US&amp;model=m3&amp;saleType=Sale\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">threatened to punish<\/a> early Cybertruck resellers, but it seemed to walk back its previously published rules in a <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/14\/tesla-hits-reverse-on-threat-to-sue-cybertruck-resellers\/\">November 2023 update<\/a> to its U.S. order agreement page. The rules once stated that Cybertruck customers couldn\u2019t sell their vehicles during their first year of ownership without permission from Tesla. The rules also said the company would seek $50,000 in damages from early resellers. But such language is gone from that particular page, at least for now.<\/p>\n<h2>Can I fit a bike in a Cybertruck\u2019s bed?<\/h2>\n<p>That depends on the size of the bike and how you store it. From what we can tell, <a href=\"https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/specializedbicycles\/posts\/718967776933761?ref=embed_post\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">you\u2019ll probably need one wheel hanging over the tailgate<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p>Elon Musk has promised that Cybertrucks will feature beds longer than six feet. The truck bed in the production version is six-feet and slightly longer with the tailgate down. From the end of one wheel to the opposite end of the other, adult bicycles are typically longer than six feet. (On a related note, the city of Los Angeles recommends bicycle parking spaces be a minimum of <a href=\"https:\/\/engpermitmanual.lacity.org\/sites\/default\/files\/documents\/Bike%20Parking%20Ordinance%20Guide_Final%20Draftr.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">six feet long<\/a>, as does South Carolina\u2019s <a href=\"https:\/\/pccsc.net\/bicycle-parking-info\/#:~:text=Bicycle%20handlebars%20are%2015%20%E2%80%93%2018,length%20is%20about%2068%20inches.\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Palmetto Cycling Coalition<\/a>. Regardless, it&#8217;s a tight fit.)<\/p>\n<p>A motorcycle would probably be trickier. This isn\u2019t a Silverado.<\/p>\n<h2>Is the Cybertruck good for the climate?<\/h2>\n<p>Generally speaking, electric vehicles <a style=\"background-color: #ffffff; font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\" href=\"https:\/\/yaleclimateconnections.org\/2022\/11\/dont-get-fooled-electric-vehicles-really-are-better-for-the-climate\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">are<\/a><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\"> better for the climate than their gas-guzzling counterparts, because they don&#8217;t have tailpipe emissions. Still, all cars are pollutive. That includes consumer EVs, since they require <a href=\"https:\/\/news.climate.columbia.edu\/2023\/01\/18\/the-paradox-of-lithium\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">lithium<\/a>, release <a href=\"https:\/\/grist.org\/transportation\/evs-are-a-climate-solution-with-a-pollution-problem-tire-particles\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">tire particles<\/a> into the air and demand more energy than more efficient means of getting around, such as public transit, cycling and walking. Battery material mining is also <a href=\"https:\/\/news.harvard.edu\/gazette\/story\/2023\/04\/benita-kayembe-takes-hard-look-at-hidden-human-cost-of-electric-cars\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">linked to worker exploitation<\/a>.\u00a0<\/span><\/p>\n<p>What about the Cybertruck&#8217;s size? Experts have raised red flags over the <a href=\"https:\/\/www.npr.org\/2023\/11\/14\/1212737005\/cars-trucks-pedestrian-deaths-increase-crash-data#:~:text=in%20Austin%2C%20Texas.-,The%20average%20U.S.%20passenger%20vehicle%20has%20gotten%20about%208%20inches,Insurance%20Institute%20for%20Highway%20Safety.&amp;text=American%20cars%20and%20trucks%20keep,a%20steep%20cost%20to%20safety.\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">ballooning size and weight<\/a> of vehicles in the U.S. Extra-large vehicles typically require <a href=\"https:\/\/www.sciencedirect.com\/science\/article\/abs\/pii\/S0301421521006121\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">more energy<\/a>, and thus more battery materials, which drives up their environmental cost. As far as collisions go, bigger and heavier vehicles are <a href=\"https:\/\/www.bloomberg.com\/news\/articles\/2023-01-12\/electric-cars-are-getting-too-big-and-bulky-safety-regulator-warns\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">less safe for everyone around them<\/a>, especially pedestrians.<\/p>\n<p>Among EVs, vehicles with smaller batteries are <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wired.com\/story\/the-earth-is-begging-you-to-accept-smaller-ev-batteries\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">generally better for the environment<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>Stay tuned<\/h2>\n<p>We have a lot more questions and we expect Tesla to share more details during its <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/innovation-or-folly-the-cybertruck-will-test-whether-anyone-still-trusts-tesla\/\">November 30 delivery event<\/a>. How does the Cybertruck compare to other Teslas? What about electric pickups from the competition, including Ford or GMC? When will Tesla release the cheaper, single-motor Cybertruck variant it promised back in 2019? Check back for updates as we learn more.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>After four years, the long-awaited launch of the Tesla Cybertruck electric pickup has come and gone. The boxy vehicle is Tesla\u2019s first new model since 2020, when it started delivering the Model Y. Yet, Cybertruck&#8217;s initial debut predates that moment; Tesla CEO Elon Musk showed off an early version of the pickup at a memorable [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574547,"featured_media":2634713,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"ce54f39a-5687-368d-b163-1740d3332b51","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-11-30T12:06:01Z","apple_news_api_id":"7e531edd-215b-4845-8cc4-09801e0fd751","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T20:19:10Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAABQ==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/AflMe3SFbSEWMxAmAHg_XUQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[576957003,2401],"tags":[576716027,197443,76801,429989],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>What&#039;s up with Tesla&#039;s Cybertruck? Everything to know about the much-hyped electric pickup | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"Here&#039;s everything to know about Tesla&#039;s Cybertruck electric pickup, including its design, specs and current availability.\" \/>\n<link rel=\"canonical\" href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/whats-up-with-teslas-cybertruck-everything-to-know-about-the-much-hyped-electric-pickup\/\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:locale\" content=\"en_US\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:type\" content=\"article\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:title\" content=\"What&#039;s up with Tesla&#039;s Cybertruck? runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=429989"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636426,"date":"2023-12-01T12:05:55","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:05:55","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636426"},"modified":"2023-11-30T17:45:31","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T01:45:31","slug":"when-uncle-sam-puts-his-thumb-on-your-brake-pedal","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/when-uncle-sam-puts-his-thumb-on-your-brake-pedal\/","title":{"rendered":"When Uncle Sam puts his thumb on your brake pedal"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><em>Welcome to Startups Weekly. Sign up <\/em><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/newsletters\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><em>here<\/em><\/a><em> to get it in your inbox every Friday.<\/em><\/p>\n<p>Hello, you scrumptious specimen of a human being. Have you looked yourself in the mirror and given yourself a high five today? No? Well, here\u2019s your chance!<\/p>\n<p>I\u2019ve had a productive couple of weeks on the site. I got pissy about founders failing to consider sustainability in startup pitch decks. I looked at a few thousand decks and realized that <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/this-is-unsustainable\/\">fewer than 1% mention environmental concerns<\/a>, a fact I find deeply concerning. I argue that startup founders, unlike individuals in large corporations, are uniquely positioned to embed values like sustainability into the very fabric of their companies. Be better.<\/p>\n<p>As I was talking with a founder last week about how they had terrible founder-market fit, and then got to thinking: Damn, it\u2019s no wonder that most of my startups weren\u2019t all that successful: <em>I<\/em> didn\u2019t have great founder-market fit. Pretty sobering, and I concluded that, well, maybe I just don\u2019t have <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/23\/yo-ho-a-founders-life-for-me\/\">what it takes to be a founder<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p>The final delightful rant I went on was in the aftermath of the whole Sam Altman\/OpenAI shenanigans, where it seemed to transpire that he &#8220;hadn\u2019t been forthcoming&#8221; with the board. That\u2019s corporate speak for suppressing information \u2014 super bad juju when it comes to the C-suite and the board. In a nutshell: No, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/22\/dont-lie-to-your-board-of-directors\/\">you can\u2019t lie to your board<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p>With your regularly scheduled Haje rants out of the way \u2014 and given that it was a turkey feast last week, so I was too deep in a meat coma to write Startups Weekly last week \u2014 we have <em>two<\/em> weeks of startup news to catch you up on. Let\u2019s get to it\u00a0.\u00a0.\u00a0.<\/p>\n<h2>Car go vroom? Not so fast.<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2632590\" style=\"width: 3010px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2632590\" class=\"wp-image-2632590 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg\" alt=\"\" width=\"3000\" height=\"2000\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg 3000w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=150,100 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=300,200 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=768,512 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=680,453 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=1536,1024 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=2048,1365 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=1200,800 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/GettyImages-545144650.jpg?resize=50,33 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 3000px) 100vw, 3000px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2632590\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Spencer Platt\/Getty Images<\/p><\/div><\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>Look, I\u2019m all for saving lives, but <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/16\/ntsb-speed-reduction-tech-in-every-new-car\/\">the government\u2019s recommendation<\/a> to make intelligent speed-assist (ISA) technology standard in new vehicles is an outrageous overreach. It is my God-given right as an American to drive 70 mph in a 65 mph zone, and I\u2019m particularly furious about the suggestion that they\u2019re going to use a car\u2019s GPS and cameras to limit your speed to the speed limits. I\u2019m all for road safety and all, but . . . come on. It isn\u2019t even like it\u2019s going to be all that helpful in the grand scheme of things: Plenty of fatal accidents happen well within the speed limits because idiots are futzing with their phones, and the number of uninsured and unlicensed drivers on the roads (not to mention the vast number of completely un-road-worthy junk heaps that are driving around) are far more dangerous than speeding.<\/p>\n<p>While rooted in the noble intent of reducing traffic fatalities, the recommendation ignites a debate that extends beyond road safety, swerving violently into the headlights of core values of personal freedom, trust in government, and the evolving relationship between technology and our everyday lives.<\/p>\n<p>Oh well, at least there\u2019s still motorcycles, I guess.<\/p>\n<p>In other news that goes vroom (or swoosh in the case of EVs):<\/p>\n<p><strong>Self-firing CEO: <\/strong>In a surprising turn of events reminiscent of a driverless car taking an unexpected detour, Kyle Vogt, the visionary behind Cruise&#8217;s journey from a humble startup to a GM powerhouse, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/19\/cruise-co-founder-and-ceo-kyle-vogt-resigns\/\">has decided to park his role as CEO<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p><strong>The Buy Now button just got more dangerous: <\/strong>Amazon, once a humble bookstore, is now revving up to steer into the car market, partnering with Hyundai to start <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/16\/amazon-to-sell-cars-online-starting-with-hyundai\/\">selling vehicles online<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Didn\u2019t see that coming: <\/strong>In a turn of events that even a self-driving car didn&#8217;t see coming, a Florida judge has found that Tesla, under the leadership of Elon Musk, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/21\/florida-judge-finds-tesla-elon-musk-knew-of-defective-autopilot-system\/\">was aware of defects in its Autopilot<\/a> system, yet continued to let them cruise down the road of public use.<\/p>\n<h2>So what was that whole Sam Altman thing?<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2631511\" style=\"width: 1034px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2631511\" class=\"size-full wp-image-2631511\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg\" alt=\"Sam Altman, Satya Nadella and Ilya Sutskever in an illustration collage\" width=\"1024\" height=\"576\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg 1920w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=150,84 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=300,169 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=768,432 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=680,383 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=1536,864 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=1200,675 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Sam-Altman-OpenAI-Nadella.jpg?resize=50,28 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2631511\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Darrell Etherington with files from Getty under license<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>In the AI world&#8217;s equivalent of a high-tech soap opera, Sam Altman, the erstwhile CEO of OpenAI, found himself ousted in a flurry of drama and intrigue. The tale begins with Altman&#8217;s dismissal by the OpenAI board, a move that sent ripples through the organization, leading to the resignation of co-founder Greg Brockman and a trio of senior researchers. As the plot thickened, Microsoft, a key investor in OpenAI, gained a seat at the table, albeit as a non-voting observer, hinting at the growing influence of larger tech entities in the AI space.<\/p>\n<p>The story took a twist worthy of a primetime cliffhanger when OpenAI and Altman reached an &#8220;agreement in principle&#8221; for his grand return as CEO, reshuffling the board with high-profile names like Bret Taylor and Larry Summers. This roller-coaster ride was not without its share of corporate drama and power plays, including failed negotiations, attempts to merge with rival AI firm Anthropic, and a mass employee threat to resign, all underscoring the high stakes and intense passions driving the AI industry. In this tale of AI ambition, the only certainty was uncertainty, as the future of one of the industry&#8217;s leading lights hung in the balance. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/a-timeline-of-sam-altmans-firing-from-openai-and-the-fallout\/\">Kyle has a great summary of what happened<\/a>, with a link to all the TechCrunch coverage and analysis. It\u2019s worth a read, because\u00a0.\u00a0.\u00a0.\u00a0woweee.<\/p>\n<p>All in all, we still don\u2019t <em>really<\/em> know what happened behind closed doors, but a memorable scene from the HBO show &#8220;The Wire&#8221; kept echoing through my mind: <a href=\"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/watch?v=aVC6h2lRt6Q&amp;t=118s\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">If you come at the king, you\u2019d best not miss<\/a> (NSFW). I can\u2019t remember a time when a CEO got booted out of the company they founded, and then ended up shaking up the entire board and being reinstated.<\/p>\n<p>Mark my words: This OpenAI saga is a Aaron Sorkin\/David Fincher\/Jesse Eisenberg movie collab waiting to happen.<\/p>\n<p>Other AI news from the last couple of weeks:<\/p>\n<p><strong>Hey, Siri, take a break: <\/strong>Move over, Siri, there&#8217;s a new voice in town! Learn how to transform <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/22\/forget-siri-turn-your-iphones-action-button-into-a-chatgpt-voice-assistant-instead\/\">your iPhone&#8217;s action button into a ChatGPT-powered assistant<\/a> that&#8217;s ready to outsmart, outchat, and outwit.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Is it AI-ll it\u2019s cracked up to be?: <\/strong>In a plot twist worthy of a tech noir, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/apple-and-google-avoid-naming-chatgpt-as-their-app-of-the-year-picking-alltrails-and-imprint-instead\/\">Apple and Google sidestepped the AI sensation ChatGPT<\/a> for their App of the Year crowns. And that makes sense \u2014 it\u2019s a competitor to core aspects of both companies\u2019 businesses \u2014\u00a0 instead crowning AllTrails and Imprint as their Apps of the Year.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Would you like chips with that?: <\/strong>Elon Musk&#8217;s Neuralink has <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/25\/neuralink-elon-musks-brain-implant-startup-quietly-raises-an-additional-43m\/\">slyly added a cool $43 million to its coffers<\/a>, proving that when it comes to funding, some companies can really think outside the skull.<\/p>\n<h2>The lows and lowers of Silicon Valley<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2633460\" style=\"width: 2058px\" class=\"wp-caption aligncenter\"><img decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2633460\" class=\"wp-image-2633460 size-full\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg\" alt=\"Teenage Engineering's retro-looking $300 groovebox, with large grey and orange buttons.\" width=\"2048\" height=\"1792\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=150,131 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=300,263 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=768,672 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=680,595 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=1536,1344 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=1200,1050 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/te.jpeg?resize=50,44 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 2048px) 100vw, 2048px\" \/><p id=\"caption-attachment-2633460\" class=\"wp-caption-text\">Teenage Engineering&#8217;s retro $300 groovebox. <strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Teenage Engineering<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>Welcome to this week&#8217;s edition of &#8220;Silicon Valley Scandals Weekly&#8221; \u2014 your one-stop shop for all the jaw-dropping tales of tech&#8217;s titans tumbling down from their digital thrones. This week, we&#8217;re serving up a juicy slice of schadenfreude pie, featuring none other than Mike Rothenberg, the latest &#8220;disgraced darling&#8221; of Silicon Valley. Connie\u2019s story reads like a <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/16\/yet-another-former-silicon-valley-darling-is-convicted-of-investor-fraud\/\">cautionary tale for ambitious venture capitalists<\/a>, with Rothenberg joining the infamous ranks of Elizabeth Holmes and Sam Bankman-Fried, with a conviction on a staggering 21 counts of fraud and money laundering.<\/p>\n<p>Rothenberg&#8217;s saga is the stuff of thrillers! From his dazzling days as a self-proclaimed math Olympian turned Harvard MBA grad, to his heady rise as the Bay Area&#8217;s party king, he&#8217;s the man who had it all\u00a0.\u00a0.\u00a0.\u00a0until he didn&#8217;t. His journey from the glittering heights of venture capital to the gloomy depths of legal woes is a testament to the age-old adage: What goes up, must come down.<\/p>\n<p>Moar Silicon Valley goss:<\/p>\n<p><strong>Ctrl + Alt + Defeat: <\/strong>Following a crippling ransomware attack, leading title insurance company Fidelity National Financial <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/ransomware-catastrophe-at-fidelity-national-financial-causes-panic-with-homeowners-and-buyers\/\">faced a catastrophe<\/a> that left its clients in a vortex of confusion and panic, with their digital lifelines cut and financial transactions in limbo.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Stay classy, AWS: <\/strong>In an era where tech titans are racing to dominate the generative AI landscape, AWS&#8217; keynote felt less like visionary leadership and more like a jibe fest. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/aws-takes-the-cheap-shots\/\">AWS CEO Adam Selipsky took cheap shots at competitors<\/a>, revealing more about AWS&#8217; insecurities than its strengths, Frederic writes.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Keeping it fresh in hardware land: <\/strong>Hrrgh, I love Teenage Engineering, and I share Harri&#8217;s excitement: <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/23\/im-trying-so-hard-not-to-gush-over-teenage-engineerings-latest-gadget\/\">The startup&#8217;s latest creation<\/a> is making it incredibly hard for us not to turn into a gushing stan \u2014 a rare feat in today&#8217;s tech landscape.<\/p>\n<h2>Top reads on TechCrunch the past two weeks<\/h2>\n<p><strong>Bailtm: <\/strong>Warren Buffett&#8217;s Berkshire Hathaway, known for its Midas touch, has made a rare stumble, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/24\/warren-buffett-berkshire-hathaway-exits-paytm-at-a-40-loss\/\">exiting its investment in Indian fintech giant Paytm<\/a> with a 40% haircut.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Lemme mansplain this one to you: <\/strong>In a world where &#8220;reply guys&#8221; lurk in every digital corner, Mastodon&#8217;s latest feature <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/22\/mastodon-tackles-the-problem-of-reply-guys-with-its-latest-feature\/\">swoops in like a superhero<\/a>, offering a shield against the unwanted chatter.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Bringing Jira down for landing: <\/strong><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/plane-takes-on-jira-with-open-source-project-management-tools-for-software-teams\/\">Plane is soaring into the project management arena<\/a>, challenging the Goliath Jira with its open source innovation. This high-flying newcomer aims to revolutionize how software teams manage projects.<\/p>\n<p><strong>Bringing in the big guns:<\/strong> Skilled AI experts are as rare as macOS kernel panics, so startups are turning to an innovative solution: <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/24\/startups-should-consider-hiring-fractional-ai-officers\/\">the fractional AI officer<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p><strong>So you say there\u2019s a way: <\/strong>Google has confessed in court that <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/20\/google-admits-spotify-pays-no-play-store-fees-because-of-a-secret-deal\/\">Spotify has been waltzing through the Play Store without paying the usual entry fees<\/a>, thanks to a hush-hush agreement.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Welcome to Startups Weekly. Sign up here to get it in your inbox every Friday. Hello, you scrumptious specimen of a human being. Have you looked yourself in the mirror and given yourself a high five today? No? Well, here\u2019s your chance! I\u2019ve had a productive couple of weeks on the site. I got pissy [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":170861,"featured_media":1497015,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"e134face-57ad-34a5-ae74-50a926c4b869","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T20:06:12Z","apple_news_api_id":"49b44f8a-f825-446a-b593-c37526d98bbc","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T20:06:12Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAD\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/w==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/ASbRPivglRGq1k8N1JtmLvA","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[20429],"tags":[144203,81,192933,1473202,449557042,576603287],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>When Uncle Sam puts his thumb on your brake pedal | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"Welcome to Startups Weekly! /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=576603287"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=576603287"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=576603287"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=576603287"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636761,"date":"2023-12-01T11:53:56","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:53:56","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636761"},"modified":"2023-12-01T12:06:01","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:06:01","slug":"amazon-signs-3-launch-deal-with-spacex-for-project-kuiper-satellite-internet-constellation","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/amazon-signs-3-launch-deal-with-spacex-for-project-kuiper-satellite-internet-constellation\/","title":{"rendered":"Amazon signs 3-launch deal with SpaceX for Project Kuiper satellite internet constellation"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Amazon has purchased three Falcon 9 launches from SpaceX to support deployment of its Project Kuiper mega-constellation, the company said Friday. The new deal comes scarcely two months after it was revealed that <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/08\/31\/lawsuit-alleges-no-due-diligence-in-amazons-project-kuiper-launch-contracts-to-blue-origin-ula\/\">Amazon was facing a lawsuit<\/a> over its decision not to consider SpaceX &#8212; the most reliable rocket company on the planet &#8212; in its first round of launch contracts.<\/p>\n<p>The three Falcon 9 missions are targeted to lift off starting in mid-2025. Kuiper is planning a constellation of 3,236 satellites in low Earth orbit; the U.S. Federal Communications Commission required Amazon to deploy at least half of that figure by 2026.<\/p>\n<p>With the clock ticking, Amazon went ahead and purchased much of the launch capacity through the middle of the decade, securing up to 83 launches from Arianespace, Blue Origin and United Launch Alliance last year at an expected cost of many billions of dollars.<\/p>\n<p>Amazon shareholders the Cleveland Bakers and Teamsters Pension Fund later filed a suit against the board over this decision, alleging that they spent less than 40 minutes approving the launch agreements while not even considering SpaceX.<\/p>\n<p>The lawsuit further claims that SpaceX was never even floated as a possibility: In July 2020, Amazon management informed its audit committee that the company was in talks with Blue Origin, Arianespace, ULA and a fourth unnamed company for Kuiper launch contracts. \u201cInexplicably, the most famous, reliable and obvious launch provider in the world \u2014 SpaceX \u2014 was not among the four companies presented to [Amazon\u2019s] Audit Committee,\u201d the complaint says.<\/p>\n<p><em>Developing\u2026 <\/em><\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Amazon has purchased three Falcon 9 launches from SpaceX to support deployment of its Project Kuiper mega-constellation, the company said Friday. The new deal comes scarcely two months after it was revealed that Amazon was facing a lawsuit over its decision not to consider SpaceX &#8212; the most reliable rocket company on the planet &#8212; [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574433,"featured_media":2367198,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"b0bb6bb3-f99d-36e9-bf3b-0ab69c1001f1","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T19:54:04Z","apple_news_api_id":"2657439c-167f-4abe-959e-3b7cf4cf271f","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T20:05:55Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/AJldDnBZ_Sr6Vnjt89M8nHw","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[174],"tags":[6602,9601187,576827282,371748],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Amazon signs 3-launch deal with SpaceX for Project Kuiper satellite internet constellation | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"Amazon has purchased three Falcon 9 launches from SpaceX to support deployment of its Project Kuiper mega-constellation, the company said Friday. runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=371748"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=371748"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=371748"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=371748"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636200,"date":"2023-12-01T11:50:35","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:50:35","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636200"},"modified":"2023-12-01T12:00:24","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:00:24","slug":"tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup\/","title":{"rendered":"Tesla Cybertruck delivery event: Everything Elon revealed about the EV pickup"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">With dubstep as the soundtrack and neon lighting as the backdrop, Elon Musk handed the first Cybertrucks over to a select group of customers that included Reddit co-founder and VC fund Seven Seven Six founder Alexis Ohanian and Trousdale Ventures founder and CEO Phillip Sarofim.<\/p>\n<p>The livestreamed portion of the <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/whats-up-with-teslas-cybertruck-everything-to-know-about-the-much-hyped-electric-pickup\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla Cybertruck delivery event<\/a> was a short affair \u2014 around 30 minutes. But the event still had all the traditional trappings one has come to expect from Tesla: the pomp and pumpy music, VIP guests and, of course, Musk.<\/p>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck deliveries come at least six years since Musk first tweeted about building a truck and four years since he debuted the futuristic-looking pickup.<\/p>\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">Looking beyond some of the flashier features \u2014 it&#8217;s bullet proof \u2014 here&#8217;s everything we know so far. The tl;dr: the Cybertruck is a lot more expensive than the targets Musk shared in 2019. And what other details were shared, were scant. A few reviews, including <a href=\"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/watch?v=XxOh12Uhg08\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">one from Marques Brownlee<\/a>, have been far more illuminating than what Musk or the official Tesla website have shared.<\/span><\/p>\n<h2>Three versions<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2636304\" style=\"width: 1034px\" class=\"wp-caption alignnone\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2636304\" class=\"size-full wp-image-2636304\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg\" alt=\"Tesla cybertruck delivery event\" width=\"1024\" height=\"477\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg 2934w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=150,70 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=300,140 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=768,358 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=680,317 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=1536,715 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=2048,953 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=1200,559 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=50,23 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2636304\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Tesla\/screenshot<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck will eventually be available in three configurations.<\/p>\n<p>The cheapest of the batch, a single-motor rear-wheel version with 250 miles of range, a 6.9-second zero to 60 miles per hour acceleration rate and a $60,990 base price, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/teslas-cheapest-cybertruck-wont-drop-until-2025\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">won&#8217;t be available until 2025<\/a>. That leaves a dual motor all-wheel version and the so-called Cyberbeast, which has three motors.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>The all-wheel drive variant has an estimated 340-mile range, top speed of 112 mph and $79,990 starting price. The Cyberbeast comes with an estimated 320-mile range model with 845 horsepower that can travel 0 to 60 miles per hour in 2.6 seconds, can hit a top speed of 130 mph, and comes with estimated $99,990 price tag. Both of these versions have a claimed towing capacity of 11,000 pounds.<\/p>\n<p>The company is also going to offer a range extender that will push the all-wheel drive version to an estimated 470 miles and the Cyberbeast to more than 440 miles of range. But Tesla provides no other details about the range extender or the pricing. After the event, Musk did take to X, formerly Twitter, to give a slightly less opaque explanation. He said the range extender will be an &#8220;optional pack that fits in about 1\/3 of the truck bed. Still room for plenty of cargo. It\u2019s meant for very long trips or towing heavy things up mountains.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>Still no word on what it will cost. Either way, any EV truck or SUV priced above $80,000 won&#8217;t qualify for the $7,500 federal tax credit. Which means that middle tier variant with a range extender probably won&#8217;t.<\/p>\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">The numbers above differ from the original specs first shared by Musk at the <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/behold-the-tesla-cybertruck-is-here\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Cybertruck debut event<\/a> in 2019. The company was planning on three variants, but the prices, towing and range have since changed. The production vehicle dimensions have also been reduced by about 5% from the prototype shown four years ago, making it slightly smaller than the Ford F-150 Lightning. <\/span><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">In 2019, the company planned for its cheapest version to cost $39,900, and have a towing capacity of 7,500 pounds and more than 250 miles of range. The middle version was slated to be a dual-motor all-wheel drive priced at $49,900, have a towing capacity of more than 10,000 pounds and be able to travel more than 300 miles on a single charge. The third version was supposed to have three electric motors and all-wheel drive, a towing capacity of 14,000 pounds and battery range of more than 500 miles. That version, known as \u201ctri motor,\u201d is priced at $69,900.<\/span><\/p>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p>Today, all of the production Cybertrucks have a drag coefficient of 0.335 (that&#8217;s good aerodynamics, fyi), 35-inch all-terrain tires on 20-inch rims, a six-foot by four-foot composite truck bed, a hidden gear locker and a front trunk. The company says that in all there is 67 cubic feet of lockable storage and it has a maximum payload of 2,500 pounds. Notably, the Cybertruck is a <span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">steer-by-wire vehicle, which should give it the ability to make tight turns and maneuvers.\u00a0<\/span><\/p>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">Also of note, Tesla switched from a 12-volt to a 48-volt electrical system like other automakers such as Mercedes-Benz and Volkswagen have done. The 48-volt system delivers more efficient power distribution and can provide enough amperage to charge things like power tools.\u00a0\u00a0<\/span><\/p>\n<\/div>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p>The Cybertruck also comes equipped with electronically adaptive air suspension that allows for up to 17 inches of clearance, if needed.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n<h2>Tesla Cybertruck exterior<\/h2>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck still has the same trapezoidal shape and stainless steel exterior as presented back in 2019. There are a few changes however. The truck bed is no longer stainless steel, but lined with a composite. And those side mirrors? They are removable.<\/p>\n<p>The exterior includes armored glass that is supposed to resist the impact of a baseball at 70 mph or class 4 hail. Testing the glass <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">didn&#8217;t work so well back in 2019,<\/a> with it actually shattering. This time chief designer Franz von Holzhausen successfully threw a baseball at the vehicle&#8217;s window without breaking it. Although it should be noted that back in 2019, von Holzhausen threw a metal baseball-sized ball. On Thursday, it appeared to be a regular old baseball and not one thrown 70 mph.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<h2>Inside the Cybertruck<\/h2>\n<p>Now that <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/innovation-or-folly-the-cybertruck-will-test-whether-anyone-still-trusts-tesla\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla Cybertrucks<\/a> are being delivered, there&#8217;s more information on some of the interior details and features.<\/p>\n<p>As Musk handed over the Cybertrucks, he pressed what looked like a button on the B pillar to open the doors. It should be noted that the backdoors open at 90 degrees, a detail that wasn&#8217;t pointed out at the event but was revealed in a few of the review videos that have since been released. Once inside, drivers and passengers might notice an 18.5-inch infinity touchscreen in the front and a 9.4-inch touchscreen in the back. Apparently, there&#8217;s also a new interface, although Tesla doesn&#8217;t elaborate on how it differs from the current version.<\/p>\n<p>Unlike the prototype, the steering wheel is a wheel and not a yoke. And another oddly placed item is the gear shifter, in this case buttons that allow the driver to move from park to reserve or drive, is placed up by where one would normally find the rearview mirror. Oh and yup, there is no rearview mirror.<\/p>\n<p>Interestingly, and as noted by Brownlee, there is a front-facing camera outside of the Cybertruck \u2014 a first in Tesla&#8217;s world \u2014 which projects an image to that center screen to help the driver see any potential obstacles.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck also comes with a 15-speaker sound system that includes two dedicated subwoofers and distributed amplifiers. A built-in HEPA air filter (also in other Tesla vehicles), a wireless charging system and 65W USB-C and 120V\/240V outlets are also included.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>With dubstep as the soundtrack and neon lighting as the backdrop, Elon Musk handed the first Cybertrucks over to a select group of customers that included Reddit co-founder and VC fund Seven Seven Six founder Alexis Ohanian and Trousdale Ventures founder and CEO Phillip Sarofim. The livestreamed portion of the Tesla Cybertruck delivery event was [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574188,"featured_media":2636252,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"a346e424-4f23-3928-8162-be8784969443","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-11-30T22:14:41Z","apple_news_api_id":"6f5619b7-f3ca-43be-8a46-295d85f2b083","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T20:00:29Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAEA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/Ab1YZt_PKQ76KRildhfKwgw","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,197443,1244446,76801,429989],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Tesla Cybertruck delivery event: Everything Elon revealed about the EV pickup | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"Tesla CEO Elon Musk handed over the first Cybertrucks to customers at an event at its Texas gigafactory. runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=429989"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636715,"date":"2023-12-01T11:24:45","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:24:45","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636715"},"modified":"2023-12-01T11:41:05","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:41:05","slug":"good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms\/","title":{"rendered":"Good old-fashioned AI remains viable in spite of the rise of LLMs"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Remember a year ago, all the way back <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/on-chatgpts-first-anniversary-its-mobile-apps-have-topped-110m-installs-and-nearly-30m-in-revenue\/\">to last November <\/a>before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? That approach seemed to go out the window with the rise of generalized LLMs, but the fact is generalized models aren\u2019t well suited to every problem, and task-based models are still alive and well in the enterprise.<\/p>\n<p>These task-based models have, up until the rise of LLMs, been the basis for most AI in the enterprise, and they aren\u2019t going away. It\u2019s what Amazon CTO Werner Vogels referred to as \u201cgood old-fashioned AI\u201d in his keynote this week, and in his view, is the kind of AI that is still solving a lot of real-world problems.<\/p>\n<p>Atul Deo, general manager of Amazon Bedrock, the product <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/09\/28\/amazon-launches-its-bedrock-generative-ai-service-in-general-availability\/\">introduced earlier this year<\/a> as a way to plug into a variety of large language models via APIs, also believes that task models aren\u2019t going to simply disappear. Instead, they have become another AI tool in the arsenal.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cBefore the advent of large language models, we were mostly in a task-specific world. And the idea there was you would train a model from scratch for a particular task,\u201d Deo told TechCrunch. He says the main difference between the task model and the LLM is that one is trained for that specific task, while the other can handle things outside the boundaries of the model.<\/p>\n<p>Jon Turow, a partner at investment firm Madrona, who formerly spent almost a decade at AWS, says the industry has been talking about emerging capabilities in large language models like reasoning and out-of-domain robustness. \u201cThese allow you to be able to stretch beyond a narrow definition of what the model was initially expected to do,\u201d he said. But, he added, it\u2019s still very much up for debate how far these capabilities can go.<\/p>\n<p>Like Deo, Turow says task models aren\u2019t simply going to suddenly go away. \u201cThere is clearly still a role for task-specific models because they can be smaller, they can be faster, they can be cheaper and they can in some cases even be more performant because they&#8217;re designed for a specific task,\u201d he said.<\/p>\n<p>But the lure of an all-purpose model is hard to ignore. \u201cWhen you&#8217;re looking at an aggregate level in a company, when there are hundreds of machine learning models being trained separately, that doesn&#8217;t make any sense,\u201d Deo said. \u201cWhereas if you went with a more capable large language model, you get the reusability benefit right away, while allowing you to use a single model to tackle a bunch of different use cases.&#8221;<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For Amazon, SageMaker, the company&#8217;s machine learning operations platform, remains a key product, one that is aimed at data scientists instead of developers, as Bedrock is.<a href=\"https:\/\/aws.amazon.com\/blogs\/machine-learning\/aws-celebrates-5-years-of-innovation-with-amazon-sagemaker\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">\u00a0It reports<\/a> tens of thousands of customers building millions of models. It would be foolhardy to give that up, and frankly just because LLMs are the flavor of the moment doesn\u2019t mean that the technology that came before won&#8217;t remain relevant for some time to come.<\/p>\n<p>Enterprise software in particular doesn\u2019t work that way. Nobody is simply tossing their significant investment because a new thing came along, even one as powerful as the current crop of large language models. It&#8217;s worth noting that Amazon did <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-sagemaker-hyperpod-makes-it-easier-to-train-and-fine-tune-llms\/\">announce upgrades to SageMaker<\/a> this week, aimed squarely at managing large language models.<\/p>\n<p>Prior to these more capable large language models, the task model was really the only option, and that\u2019s how companies approached it, by building a team of data scientists to help develop these models. What is the role of the data scientist in the age of large language models where tools are being aimed at developers? Turow thinks they still have a key job to do, even in companies concentrating on LLMs.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cThey&#8217;re going to think critically about data, and that is actually a role that is growing, not shrinking,\u201d he said. Regardless of the model, Turow believes data scientists will help people understand the relationship between AI and data inside large companies.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cI think every one of us needs to really think critically about what AI is and is not capable of and what data does and does not mean,\u201d he said. And that\u2019s true regardless of whether you\u2019re building a more generalized large language model or a task model.<\/p>\n<p>That&#8217;s why these two approaches will continue to work concurrently for some time to come because sometimes bigger is better, and sometimes it\u2019s not.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/aws-reinvent-2023\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-reinvent-2023-banner.jpg\" alt=\"Read more about AWS re:Invent 2023 on TechCrunch\" \/><\/a><\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Remember a year ago, all the way back to last November before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? Roundup: Forestry tech is at an inflection point"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Forests are worth as much as $150 trillion, according to the Boston Consulting Group, with much of the interest in them centering around carbon credits. That means that forests that are conserved or restored need to be monitored, to make sure that everything is working as planned. But that data is often unreliable and gathering it is tedious, leaving the door wide open for startups in the space.<\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p style=\"text-align: center;\"><i>Full TechCrunch+ articles are only available to members.<br \/>\n<a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/subscribe\/\">Use discount code <strong>TCPLUSROUNDUP<\/strong><\/a> to save 20% off a one- or two-year subscription.<\/i><\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p>\u201cThe hope is that improving data quality and cost-effective monitoring will help increase the value of a conserved or restored forest,&#8221; writes Tim De Chant in our most recent survey of climate tech investors.<\/p>\n<p>Thanks for reading,<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/twitter.com\/karynelevy\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Karyne<\/a><\/p>\n<div
