authors and their 8 gift book picks for this holiday season"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><span class=\"featured__span-first-words\">Book blurbs have<\/span> become a central part of the publishing industry: Who better to endorse a book than other authors and thought leaders? In that same spirit, we asked several writers to recommend books that you and other TechCrunch readers may want to gift this holiday season.<\/p>\n<p>Read on for recommendations from:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Jon Evans, author of <b>\u201c<\/b><a href=\"https:\/\/rezendi.com\/wp\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Exadelic<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Kashmir Hill, author of <b>\u201c<\/b><a href=\"https:\/\/www.kashmirhill.com\/book\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Your Face Belongs to Us: A Secretive Startup&#8217;s Quest to End Privacy as We Know It<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Jerry Neumann, co-author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.foundervsinvestor.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Founder vs Investor: The Honest Truth About Venture Capital from Startup to IPO<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Barr Moses, co-author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.montecarlodata.com\/oreilly-data-quality-fundamentals-early-release\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Data Quality Fundamentals: A Practitioner&#8217;s Guide to Building Trustworthy Data Pipelines<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Polina Marinova Pompliano, author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.hiddengeniusbook.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Hidden Genius: The secret ways of thinking that power the world&#8217;s most successful people<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Georgiana Laudi, co-author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Forget-Funnel-Customer-Led-Predictable-Recurring\/dp\/B0C3FNS8JG\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Forget the Funnel: A Customer-Led Approach for Driving Predictable, Recurring Revenue<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">Scott Hurff, author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/gp\/product\/1491923679\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Designing Products People Love: How Great Designers Create Successful Products<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<li aria-level=\"1\">James Wise, author of \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.co.uk\/Start-Up-Century-becoming-entrepreneurs-everyone\/dp\/1399410598\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Start-up Century: Why We&#8217;re All Becoming Entrepreneurs &#8211; and How to Make It Work for Everyone<\/a>\u201d<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p><i>The responses have been lightly edited for length and clarity. This article contains links to affiliate partners where available. When you buy through these links, TechCrunch may earn an affiliate commission.<\/i><\/p>\n<h2><b>Jon Evans<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>You may know Jon Evans as a former <\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/author\/jon-evans\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>TechCrunch columnist<\/i><\/a><i> or from his engineering and CTO roles, but he is also a <\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/rezendi.com\/wp\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>novelist<\/i><\/a><i>. Most of his books are technothrillers painting travelers caught in tricky situations in exotic locations. &#8220;Exadelic&#8221; is different, and not just because it&#8217;s his first novel in over a decade: It is set in Silicon Valley, where a tech exec gets threatened by a rogue AI.<\/i><\/p>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"f0qJlVuXlH\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/09\/11\/exadelic-takes-a-shot-at-being-silicon-valleys-ready-player-one\/\">&#8216;Exadelic&#8217; takes a shot at being Silicon Valley&#8217;s &#8216;Ready Player One&#8217;<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;&#8216;Exadelic&#8217; takes a shot at being Silicon Valley&#8217;s &#8216;Ready Player One&#8217;&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/09\/11\/exadelic-takes-a-shot-at-being-silicon-valleys-ready-player-one\/embed\/#?secret=UwRMMpEmw3#?secret=f0qJlVuXlH\" data-secret=\"f0qJlVuXlH\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Steerswoman-Rosemary-Kirstein\/dp\/0991354680?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=49fe82071db1cae259c533ce6a7fc655&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">The Steerswoman<\/a>,\u201d by Rosemary Kirstein<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>I love books which turn out to be far deeper, richer, and more thought-provoking than they originally seem, and the series of novels which begin with The Steerswoman are my go-to example of just that. Read just the first few chapters, and you&#8217;ll think you &#8220;know&#8221; you&#8217;re reading a clich\u00e9d fantasy novel, albeit better-written and more feminist than most; two adventurer women meet in a medieval-ish tavern, in a land plagued by dragons, and commence a quest to discover the source of mysterious &#8211; some say magical &#8211; gems. You might be a bit surprised by the asides in which they begin to discuss math and physics, but still, clearly a fairly forgettable genre fantasy story &#8230; right?<\/p>\n<p>&#8230;Wrong. Very, very wrong. Instead (and I&#8217;m sorry for spoilering this, but it&#8217;s impossible to write about this series without either doing so or being maddeningly elliptic) these books are some of the least fantastic, most meticulously thought-out science fiction you will ever read. More, they are a meditation on science itself, on how and why we acquire knowledge, on the repercussions of that knowledge being hoarded rather than shared &#8230; and on the inevitable(?) inequities, oppressions, and breakdowns between communities that result.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>They&#8217;re also a whole lot of fun! Not just an ongoing puzzle to be solved, but a series of genuinely thrilling, funny, and wrenching adventures, populated by great characters, to experience from the edge of your seat. Read the first, and if you like it &#8212; and I bet you will &#8212; lucky you; the sequels are even better.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>Kashmir Hill<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>Kashmir Hill&#8217;s book \u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/gp\/product\/0593448561\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Your Face Belong to Us<\/i><\/a><i>\u201d is the story of a &#8220;small AI company that gave facial recognition to law enforcement, billionaires and businesses, threatening to end privacy as we know it.&#8221; But she doesn&#8217;t need to resort to (science) fiction to make it chilling; the company in question is Clearview AI, which very much exists, and its Dutch publisher describes the book as a &#8220;real-life thriller.&#8221;<\/i><\/p>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"D67q9xUXDI\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/clearview-wins-ico-appeal\/\">Selfie-scraper, Clearview AI, wins appeal against UK privacy sanction<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;Selfie-scraper, Clearview AI, wins appeal against UK privacy sanction&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/clearview-wins-ico-appeal\/embed\/#?secret=xrzQQxW5mE#?secret=D67q9xUXDI\" data-secret=\"D67q9xUXDI\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Listeners-History-Wiretapping-United-States\/dp\/0674249283?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=e4778074426de469c0549a67d85648b8&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">The Listeners: A History of Wiretapping in the United States<\/a>,\u201d by Brian Hochman<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>I\u2019m fascinated by the intersection of privacy and technology and how we navigate the clashes between the two. Hochman, a professor of American Studies at Georgetown, looks back to a historical moment when society was confronted with an intrusive new technology\u2014tiny recording devices and wiretaps\u2014and how new norms and laws developed to address it. To the extent that past is prologue, I found it very compelling for framing my expectations for what\u2019s to come with the newest and most shocking forms of mass data collection and use.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>Jerry Neumann<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>\u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.foundervsinvestor.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Founder vs Investor<\/i><\/a><i>,\u201d a recently published non-fiction book, crosses two perspectives on venture-backed startups; entrepreneur Elizabeth Zalman is the founder, and veteran VC Jerry Neumann is the investor. Together, they share insights on how both sides can best work with one another.<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/How-Data-Happened-History-Algorithms\/dp\/1324006730?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=f1a89c4b4c6cc55e516e4a25f6242ed0&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">How Data Happened: A History from the Age of Reason to the Age of Algorithms<\/a>,\u201d by Chris Wiggins and Matthew L. Jones<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>Ten years ago data was &#8220;the new oil&#8221;. Now it&#8217;s something more\u2026 read the tech news and you have to wonder if anything else even matters. As a humanist, I hope so, but I don&#8217;t think you can reason about that unless you know the history. &#8220;How Data Happened&#8221; is that history.<\/p>\n<p>Wiggins and Jones cover the idea of data from the advent of statistics through today. The book is well-researched, as you&#8217;d expect from a couple of Columbia&#8217;s top professors, but it&#8217;s also an interesting and engaging read. It&#8217;s a perfect gift for anyone who wants to know how our data-driven society got to where we are today and where it might take us tomorrow.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>Barr Moses<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>Barr Moses is the CEO and co-founder of <\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/monte-carlo\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Monte Carlo<\/i><\/a><i>, a data observability startup. She also co-wrote a technical book on the topic: O&#8217;Reilly&#8217;s \u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.montecarlodata.com\/oreilly-data-quality-fundamentals-early-release\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Data Quality Fundamentals: A Practitioner&#8217;s Guide to Building Trustworthy Data Pipelines<\/i><\/a><i>,\u201d sharing advice on achieving reliable data at scale.<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Dare-Lead-Brave-Conversations-Hearts\/dp\/0399592520?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=5db6aa0b0404e11d6f7f7aa3711f3c31&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Dare to Lead: Brave Work. Tough Conversations. Whole Hearts<\/a>,\u201d by Bren\u00e9 Brown<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>Reading a Bren\u00e9 Brown book is like tackling the Sunday crossword puzzle \u2013 equal parts challenging and rejuvenating \u2013 and Dare to Lead is no different.<\/p>\n<p>Dare to Lead pulls on Brown\u2019s decades of research, interviews, and experience as a professor at the University of Houston speaking with CEOs, founders, and other executives to understand what great leadership looks like and how to achieve it. She distills four key skill sets great leaders possess and highlights ways to empower employees to be courageous in the face of adversity and change.<\/p>\n<p>Published in 2018 before the Gen AI wave hit, Dare to Lead also addresses what human leaders have to bring to the table that AI and ML currently don\u2019t &#8211; empathy and connection.<\/p>\n<p>Bonus: the book also provides helpful resources to assess leadership strengths and growth opportunities that can make for a fun and informative team building activity.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>Polina Marinova Pompliano<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>It&#8217;s her newsletter <a href=\"https:\/\/theprofile.substack.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">The Profile<\/a> that led Polina Marinova Pompliano to become an author. &#8220;After years of profiling hundreds of fascinating people, I asked myself: What distinguishes the great from the truly exceptional?&#8221; This led to her first book, \u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.hiddengeniusbook.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Hidden Genius: The Secret Ways of Thinking That Power the World&#8217;s Most Successful People<\/i><\/a><i>.&#8221;<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Its-What-Do-Photographers-Life\/dp\/1472120477?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=873dae17992f9d719d830bfff3283d17&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">It&#8217;s What I Do: A Photographer&#8217;s Life of Love and War<\/a>,\u201d by Lynsey Addario<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>War photojournalist Lynsey Addario has covered every major conflict and humanitarian crisis on the planet. As she sees the destruction and the pain through the lens of her camera, her images translate that intense emotion to people across the globe.<\/p>\n<p>Her memoir &#8216;It\u2019s What I Do&#8217; is the story of how she risks her life to tell the stories of ordinary people living in extraordinarily dangerous places. In the chaotic times we&#8217;re living through right now, it&#8217;s a memoir that documents the human cost of war.<\/p>\n<p>Despite witnessing so much tragedy and brutality, she has never lost her ability to see the good in humanity. My favorite quote from the book is: \u201cI choose to live in peace and witness war\u2014to experience the worst in people but to remember the beauty.\u201d<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>Georgiana Laudi<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>Georgiana (Gia) Laudi helps SaaS businesses grow through her agency, Forget the Funnel. This is also the title of <\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Forget-Funnel-Customer-Led-Predictable-Recurring\/dp\/B0C3FNS8JG\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>the book she co-wrote with her co-founder Claire Suellentrop<\/i><\/a><i>, and whose premise is to present readers with a &#8220;a customer-led approach for driving predictable, recurring revenue.\u201d<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Loved-Rethink-Marketing-Products-Silicon\/dp\/1119703646?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=1ea361ec24c0722e4dec8751c06add04&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Loved: How to Rethink Marketing for Tech Products<\/a>,\u201d by Martina Lauchengco<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>Product Marketing, particularly in tech, is one of the most foundational and yet also somehow wildly misunderstood disciplines. \u201cLoved\u201d by Martina Lauchengco is exactly the comprehensive guide the startup scene so desperately needed.<\/p>\n<p>She manages to strike the perfect blend of accessible and comprehensive so that everyone from founders to practitioners understand the critical role product marketing plays in successful companies and what good looks like at every stage of growth.<\/p>\n<p>Though I think what I appreciate the most about this book is that when it comes to marketing and driving product adoption, even though she introduces four fundamentals of product marketing, the first and most critical to get right is customer insight. This is almost unheard of given the target market for this book is an industry where engineering and scale reign supreme.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"LBq0ZHXBTw\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2018\/12\/27\/why-your-startup-shouldnt-rush-to-1-million-in-revenue\/\">Why your startup shouldn\u2019t rush to $1 million in revenue<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;Why your startup shouldn\u2019t rush to $1 million in revenue&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2018\/12\/27\/why-your-startup-shouldnt-rush-to-1-million-in-revenue\/embed\/#?secret=zwhhovuosD#?secret=LBq0ZHXBTw\" data-secret=\"LBq0ZHXBTw\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<h2><b>Scott Hurff<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>As a writer, <\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.scotthurff.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Scott Hurff <\/i><\/a><i>doesn&#8217;t always write about product design; but when he does he is able to share his perspective as someone who&#8217;s also a product maker and designer. It is also this intersection that nurtured his book, \u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/gp\/product\/1491923679\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Designing Products People Love: How Great Designers Create Successful Products<\/i><\/a><i>.\u201d\u00a0<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Getting-Real-Smarter-Successful-Application\/dp\/0578012812?&amp;linkCode=ll1&amp;tag=techc08f-20&amp;linkId=125776a251a27f80b2345d7129a49f78&amp;language=en_US&amp;ref_=as_li_ss_tl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Getting Real: The Smarter, Faster, Easier Way to Build a Successful Web Application<\/a>,\u201d by Jason Fried, David Heinemeier Hansson and Matthew Linderman<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>\u201cGetting Real\u201d is an absolute treasure from 2006 and it&#8217;s still teaching me things. It was the first time anyone building in modern tech put together a practical, straightforward, all-encompassing guide for conceiving, building, shipping, and marketing web apps. Jason and David&#8217;s words are efficient when they need to be, detailed when it matters, and packed full of nuance that&#8217;s both unique and endearing.<\/p>\n<p>The book feels like it started as an internal series of posts or a handbook to help scale 37signals beyond the core team (from what I remember, Basecamp launched in 2004 and had quickly found success). Whatever the format, what lends \u201cGetting Real\u201d authenticity is the fact that 37signals used these exact techniques to ship Basecamp and find its customer base.<\/p>\n<p>To this day, I&#8217;ve never read anything like it. It&#8217;s one of those books that you can finish in an afternoon but stays with you for far longer, morphing into a reference book for the times when you need some extra perspective.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<h2><b>James Wise<\/b><\/h2>\n<p><i>You may know James Wise as a partner at VC firm Balderton Capital; but this London-based VC also wrote his first book, \u201c<\/i><a href=\"https:\/\/www.amazon.co.uk\/Start-Up-Century-becoming-entrepreneurs-everyone\/dp\/1399410598\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><i>Start-up Century: Why We&#8217;re All Becoming Entrepreneurs &#8211; and How to Make It Work for Everyone<\/i><\/a><i>.\u201d Launched in the U.K. on November 23, it should make a great gift for aspiring entrepreneurs around you.<\/i><\/p>\n<h3>Book recommendation: \u201c<a href=\"https:\/\/www.the-coming-wave.com\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">The Coming Wave: Technology, Power, and the 21st Century&#8217;s Greatest Dilemma<\/a>,\u201d by Mustafa Suleyman with Michael Bhaskar<\/h3>\n<blockquote><p>Mustafa has been at the heart of developments in AI for the last decade, through his work at Deepmind and now at Inflection.AI. His insights into the huge potential of AI and synthetic biology, two technologies that can evolve on their own, provide both optimism for their potential but stark warnings about the need for containment.<\/p>\n<p>In this well researched book, he and co-author Michael Bhaskar navigate the hype and hysteria surrounding AI to offer a sober account of the coming wave of technological change.<\/p><\/blockquote>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"pEptlQnmCw\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/01\/20\/new-greylock-venture-partner-mustafa-suleyman-is-looking-for-ais-next-best-thing\/\">New Greylock venture partner Mustafa Suleyman is looking for AI\u2019s next best thing<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;New Greylock venture partner Mustafa Suleyman is looking for AI\u2019s next best thing&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/01\/20\/new-greylock-venture-partner-mustafa-suleyman-is-looking-for-ais-next-best-thing\/embed\/#?secret=8nj6wb2Z6M#?secret=pEptlQnmCw\" data-secret=\"pEptlQnmCw\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Book blurbs have become a central part of the publishing industry: Who better to endorse a book than other authors and thought leaders? In that same spirit, we asked several writers to recommend books that you and other TechCrunch readers may want to gift this holiday season. Read on for recommendations from: Jon Evans, author [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574450,"featured_media":2623332,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"25b32ab5-e384-3107-971f-be5c69e81c69","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T16:05:38Z","apple_news_api_id":"d30d61dc-e18c-4e3d-bcff-447a175eb6aa","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T16:05:38Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAD\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/w==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/apple.news\/A0w1h3OGMTj28_0R6F162qg","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[577030456],"tags":[178,577196528,449587],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=449587"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=449587"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=449587"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=449587"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636577,"date":"2023-12-01T07:50:51","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T15:50:51","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636577"},"modified":"2023-12-01T08:31:21","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T16:31:21","slug":"the-end-of-elon","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/the-end-of-elon\/","title":{"rendered":"The end of Elon"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically over budget and far beyond its original shipping timeline. Plus, just a couple days earlier Musk had essentially sealed the dismal fate of another of his companies by<a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/elon-musk-says-go-fuck-yourself-to-advertisers-leaving-x\/\"> publicly telling advertising partners to &#8220;go fuck [themselves].&#8221;<\/a><\/p>\n<p>To say he was looking diminished is putting it mildly.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck launch, like most of Tesla&#8217;s delivery events, was not a substantive affair. My more patient colleagues have done a much better job actually <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup\/\">recounting what happened there than I ever could<\/a>. Suffice it to say that Musk touted his gaudy stainless steel monstrosity&#8217;s abilities for a while before explaining to a handful of the sycophantic first new Cybertruck owners (including one Alexis Ohanian) the extremely non-obvious way to open their doors and then repeating a vague and perplexing statement about how wild it&#8217;s going to be to see these driving around before calling it a day.<\/p>\n<p>I mentioned the odd choice to stand on the truck bed in semi-darkness despite having a stage lighting setup very clearly designed for him not to do that, but I neglected to note the moment when a sheepish Franz von Holzhausen was drug out to weakly toss a baseball at the Cybertruck&#8217;s windows, in a moment that seemed like unintended satire of when he <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\">smashed the windows of the original prototype at a reveal event in 2019<\/a>. The only takeaway from this watered down reenactment was that if von Holzhausen had replicated the 2019 mishap faithfully, with steel balls and a proper toss, I&#8217;m pretty much convinced he would&#8217;ve smashed the windows again.<\/p>\n<p>Musk didn&#8217;t address his crumbling social media product or how his frequent, inflammatory comments and amplification of false and dangerous conspiracy theories have made it impossible to see a path forward for X that doesn&#8217;t end badly. He didn&#8217;t talk about how his range of distractions, including a repeatedly exploding spacecraft he&#8217;s periodically launching from south Texas, are giving Tesla investors some serious misgivings. Mostly, he talked about the Cybertruck&#8217;s potential benefits to owners in case of the end of modern civilization. And again: about how cool they&#8217;ll look.<\/p>\n<p>No longer the confident, if controversial, prognosticator and big swing solutions engineer, Musk has instead become a mad-eyed, rambling and raving advocate of far-right nonsense and vacillating convictions. Even his seemingly existential objection to OpenAI&#8217;s fast-paced development of AI ended up being little more than petty jealousy, as evidenced by the harried introduction of Grok, his own, far more puerile and muddy-minded equivalent.<\/p>\n<p>Undoubtedly, Musk continues to benefit from legions of devoted fanboys &#8212; many of whom I&#8217;m sure will be letting me know how amazing he still is and how garbage I still am &#8212; but it&#8217;s hard to deny any longer that his influence is on the wane, and the crowning moment of his stupidest idea made flesh in the form of the Cybertruck seems like a meaningful bookend on his era of power.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>&nbsp;<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":5302483,"featured_media":2636578,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"7552398d-df8c-3fa2-92c3-e57ab2c9aeca","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"","apple_news_api_id":"","apple_news_api_modified_at":"","apple_news_api_revision":"","apple_news_api_share_url":"","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,1244446,429989,576716026],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- the next steps towards growing your business.","blocks":[{"title":"The TechCrunch Annual Founder Summit","description":"If you are in the first stages of growing your company, have built a product but don\u2019t know how to monetize, or who might have an idea but aren\u2019t sure where to find the resources to turn it into a viable business,  you'll want to be at TechCrunch Early Stage in Boston. You'll get the building blocks you need to understand what your next steps are to fully realizing your entrepreneurial potential and become part of the TechCrunch community of founders and builders.","text_thumbnails":[{"title":"Founder-Forward Workshops","description":"With expert-led sessions, you'll get a full day of actionable advice and strategies you can immediately put into practice. in Boston. You'll get the building blocks you need to understand what your next steps are to fully realizing your entrepreneurial potential and become part of the TechCrunch community of founders and builders.","text_thumbnails":[{"title":"Founder-Forward Workshops","description":"With expert-led sessions, you'll get a full day of actionable advice and strategies you can immediately put into practice. If you're interested in learning more about how your company can be a part TechCrunch Early Stage, get in touch with our sales team below.","button_caption":"Get in Touch","button_link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/http:\/\/info.techcrunch.com\/SponsorshipsInterest.html","button_link_open_new_window":1,"uid":"e1ecda6c-c33e-4baf-a1f0-d6036a82a17d","panels_info":{"class":"TC\\Page_Builder\\Events\\Full_Width_Image_Block","raw":false,"grid":0,"cell":0,"id":8,"style":{"background_image_attachment":2252204,"background_display":"tile"}},"type":"full-width-image","backgroundImageURL":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/12\/best-2021-tc-banner.jpg","nav_title":"","display":true},{"uid":"8538bc31-baf1-4f89-a067-f2fac4691a91","panels_info":{"class":"TC\\Page_Builder\\Events\\Newsletter_Sign_Up_Block","raw":false,"grid":0,"cell":0,"id":9,"style":{"background_image_attachment":false,"background_display":"tile"}},"type":"event-newsletter","backgroundImageURL":null,"title":"Register Your Interest","nav_title":"","display":true},{"uid":"3ecd07ba-bcfb-4116-a6c8-83d75b5f900c","panels_info":{"class":"TC\\Page_Builder\\Events\\Location_Block","raw":false,"grid":0,"cell":0,"id":10,"style":{"background_image_attachment":false,"background_display":"tile"}},"type":"map","backgroundImageURL":null,"title":"Location","nav_title":"","display":true}],"pages":[{"id":2630859,"state":["pre","current","archive"],"title":"Partners","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/events\/tc-early-stage-2024\/partners\/","redirect":""}]},"speakers":[{"bio":"<p>Ajay Agarwal is a partner at Bain Capital Ventures. For over 20 years at BCV, Ajay has worked alongside exceptional early-stage founders, supporting them from seed to growth to build iconic companies. Ajay invests in early-stage application software companies. Within apps, Ajay has spent time in the modern sales and marketing stack; vertical SAAS with embedded fintech; supply chain and logistics; and product-led growth. Prior to joining BCV, Ajay was on the early team at Trilogy Software, where he ran product and sales for eight years until the company reached $300M in revenue. He holds a BS in Electrical Engineering from Stanford University and an MBA from Harvard Business School.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/03\/Ajay-Agarwal.jpg","company":"Bain Capital Ventures","featured":0,"name":"Ajay Agarwal","uuid":"183fb02eb55a396e2c09e4de73defccc","cb_tag":212783629,"position":"Partner","links":{"crunchbase":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.crunchbase.com\/person\/ajay-agarwal","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/http:\/\/twitter.com\/ajay_bcv","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/http:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/pub\/ajay-agarwal\/0\/20\/168"}},{"bio":"<p>Pablo Dominguez joined Insight in 2018 as an Operating Partner. Pablo focuses on partnering with Insight\u2019s portfolio companies to build and scale effective commercial teams through the application of proven and repeatable go-to-market and operational best practices. He brings over 20 years of experience working on Salesforce effectiveness engagements across startups and various global 2000 companies. Prior to Insight, Pablo was the Vice President of Global Business Operations and Customer Experience at NYC-based ad tech startup AppNexus, which was acquired by Xandr (division of AT&amp;T) in 2018 for approximately $2B. Pablo joined AppNexus in 2014 and was responsible for building a Business Operations organization which provided support for Sales, Services, Marketing, and Product Line teams as well as driving annual strategic planning and key initiatives. Previously, Pablo spent four years at ADP, first standing up a Global Sales Compensation function and ultimately running Global Sales Operations across 10 business units. Prior to ADP, Pablo held various leadership roles in sales operations across Avaya, where he led the global sales integration of Avaya\u2019s $1B acquisition of Nortel Enterprise in 2009. Earlier, he began his career in management consulting at The Alexander Group, a boutique consulting firm focused on providing revenue growth solutions. Pablo received a BBA from the McCombs School of Business at The University of Texas, and his MBA from NYU\u2019s Stern School of Business. Originally from Mexico, Pablo grew up in Texas and has spent the last 16 years living in New Jersey with his wife and two sons.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/02\/Pablo-Dominguez.jpg","company":"Insight Partners","featured":0,"name":"Pablo Dominguez","uuid":"1ab85d1c458b7249ceb9613e7b19f9951677035251","cb_tag":0,"position":"Operating Partner, Sales & Customer Success","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/pabtexas","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/in\/pabtexas\/"}},{"bio":"<p>Dayna Grayson is Co-founder and General Partner of Construct Capital, an early stage venture firm that invests in extraordinary founders building technology to transform the most foundational industries of our economy from manufacturing to mobility. Dayna was one of the first venture capitalists to turn her attention to transforming these sectors of our economy through software based models. She backed companies creating new advances in manufacturing, automation, and vertically integrated consumer brands. During her time as a partner at NEA (2012-2020), she was the lead investor from the earliest stages and was on the board of companies including Desktop Metal (2015 - today), Tulip (2017 - 2020), Onshape (acqd by PTC) (2013-2019), and Framebridge (acqd by Graham Holdings) (2014-2020) among others. She also led investments in Guideline, Formlabs, Evenly and Neuralmagic. At Construct, along with her co-founder Rachel Holt, Dayna is exclusively focused on early stage investments and investing behind the accelerating changes in foundational industries that together make up half our economy\u2019s GDP are failing to meet customer expectations. Some of their investments include Copia, Veho, Hadrian, The Rounds, and Verve Motion. Dayna started her career in product development and led design efforts at Blackbaud [BLKB], the leading global provider of software to nonprofit organizations, as the company grew to over $130 million in revenue and completed a successful public offering and was an investor at North Bridge Venture Partners from 2007-2012. She is a graduate of the University of Virginia (Systems Engineering) and Harvard Business School, where she serves as a venture partner today.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/03\/Dayna-Grayson.jpg","company":"Construct Capital","featured":0,"name":"Dayna Grayson","uuid":"1da67a750df511d2a9390b4efd570688","cb_tag":447873041,"position":"Co-founder & Managing Partner","links":{"crunchbase":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.crunchbase.com\/person\/dayna-grayson","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/daynagrayson","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/pub\/dayna-grayson\/0\/750\/15\/"}},{"bio":"<p>Meghan is the Co-Founder and CEO of a stealth technology startup. She previously served as EVP of Platform at Oscar Health (NYSE: OSCR), where she oversaw engineering, product, data, marketing, clinical, ops, and the company\u2019s enterprise healthtech business. During her tenure, Meghan led a team of over 1,000 through +100% growth and IPO, and expanded access to Oscar tech and services to over 1 million members. Before joining Oscar, Meghan served in several leadership roles at Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE: UBER), including the Regional General Manager of the United States &amp; Canada. She formerly served the United States Department of Treasury as Senior Policy Advisor on Domestic Finance. She began her career at Bain Capital and Bain &amp; Company. Meghan serves as Director and Chair of the Nominating and Governance Committee of The Boston Beer Company (NYSE: SAM), and Director of Guardant Health (NASDAQ: GH). She is a Member of the Board Executive Committee and Chair of the Talent &amp; Culture Committee of WBUR, and a Member of the Board of Trustees at UP Education Network. Meghan previously served as Co-chair of the Boston Chapter of the Brookings Society and is a former Member of the Graduate Board of CityStep. Meghan attended Harvard College, graduating with her BA in the history of science in 2006 and her MBA from HBS in 2013.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/03\/Meghan-Joyce.jpg","company":"stealth technology startup","featured":0,"name":"Meghan Joyce","uuid":"5a34a32ee97fd6ce10917a0e3c1943da1679347482","cb_tag":0,"position":"Co-founder & CEO","links":{"crunchbase":""}},{"bio":"<p>Kyle leads OpenView\u2019s Growth Team, responsible for advising portfolio executives on strategies to increase revenue growth and dominate their markets. He specializes in pricing &amp; packaging strategy, which is the most effective yet overlooked growth lever at a SaaS company\u2019s disposal. He\u2019s an expert in product-led growth (PLG), go-to-market strategy, usage-based business models and SaaS benchmarks. Subscribe to his newsletter, Growth Unhinged, at kylepoyar.substack.com.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2022\/02\/Kyle-Poyar.jpg","company":"OpenView","featured":0,"name":"Kyle Poyar","uuid":"76f2e0234137d435b1af0f316985961c1644262507","cb_tag":0,"position":"Operating Partner","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/search?q=poyark"}},{"bio":"<p>Amber Qui\u00f1ones is a former fitness instructor and startup operator turned VC. In her current role as the Head of Platform &amp; Operations at BBG Ventures, Amber is the connective tissue between founders, investors, and senior operators, working to source and grow women- and BIPOC-founded businesses while increasing fund value. Prior to joining BBGV, Amber led Growth &amp; Customer Success on the founding team of the proptech startup, withco \u2014 developing the company\u2019s early product and customer journey. As a Stanford d.school grad, Amber cares deeply about user experience and service design; and her personal mission is to elevate diverse leaders creating delightful, need-solving products that support underestimated communities.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/02\/Amber-Quinones.jpg","company":"BBG Ventures","featured":0,"name":"Amber Qui\u00f1ones","uuid":"8f1e04d64b023413a360b97cf67a10701677035519","cb_tag":0,"position":"Head of Platform & Operations","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/amberquinoa?lang=en","linkedin":"linkedin.com\/in\/amberquinones"}},{"bio":"<p>Matt Segneri is the Bruce and Bridgitt Evans Executive Director of the Harvard Innovation Labs, which includes the student i-lab, Launch Lab X GEO for alumni, and the Pagliuca Harvard Life Lab. He oversees the i-lab\u2019s strategy and operations and acts as the primary liaison to the i-lab\u2019s key stakeholders and the local, national, and global innovation community. He has 20+ years of leadership experience in organizations of different sizes and stages of development across the private, public, and non-profit sectors. Prior to joining the i-lab, he was the Director of the HBS Social Enterprise Initiative. During that time, he served as co-lead of the HBS New Venture Competition and played a key role in developing the proposal that established the Bloomberg Harvard City Leadership Initiative. Previously, he was a senior leader on the Government Innovation team at Bloomberg Philanthropies. He co-led the inaugural Mayors Challenge, a $9 million competition to inspire American cities to develop bold solutions to common challenges, and oversaw initiatives on cross-sector collaboration and innovation teams around the world He holds an A.B. in Psychology from Harvard College and an MBA from Harvard Business School. He was recognized by the Boston Business Journal as one of Boston\u2019s \u201c40 Under 40\u201d and the Greater Boston Chamber of Commerce as one of Boston\u2019s \u201cTen Outstanding Young Leaders.\u201d He is the Vice Chair of the National Board of Directors for Generation Citizen and serves as an advisor to New Politics and Project 351. He lives in Newton with his wife and three children.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/02\/Matt-Segneri.jpg","company":"Harvard Innovation Labs","featured":0,"name":"Matt Segneri","uuid":"de08870c3aaac65d59ec02b70dbdc95b1675903031","cb_tag":0,"position":"Bruce & Bridgitt Evans Executive Director","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/MattSegneri","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/in\/segneri\/"}},{"bio":"<p>Naomi Smith is an associate in the Boston office of Latham &amp; Watkins and a member of the Emerging Companies Practice. Ms. Smith represents startups and other emerging growth companies at all stages in a broad range of transactions including: venture capital financings, general corporate formation and governance matters, debt and equity securities offerings, mergers and acquisitions, private equity investments. She advises clients on a variety of transactions within the technology, healthcare, and life sciences industries, in addition to a number of leading venture capital firms.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/04\/Naomi-Smith-Latham.jpeg","company":"Latham & Watkins LLP","featured":0,"name":"Naomi Smith","uuid":"372c50323c0f21f4b2d006e36bce188d1680554695","cb_tag":577130591,"position":"Associate","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/lathamwatkins","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/company\/latham-&-watkins"}},{"bio":"<p>David is an experienced technology leader who has built out new businesses and products for some of the most iconic companies in the world. Immediately prior to joining Greylock, David served as VP of Product Management &amp; User Experience at Google. And before that, David managed products across LinkedIn\u2019s business lines including Premium Subscriptions, Talent Solutions, Marketing Solutions and Sales Solutions. At Greylock, he is focused on investing in founders building applications - productivity tools, front office software, and vertical SaaS - along with marketplaces and gaming. His investments include companies like Magical, Instawork, Curated, and Pragma.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/01\/David-Thacker.jpg","company":"Greylock","featured":0,"name":"David Thacker","uuid":"ac9488ede825a11bd4a1443192b34ce31617901239","cb_tag":576871640,"position":"General Partner","links":{"crunchbase":"","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/davthack","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/in\/thackerd"}},{"bio":"<p>Russ Wilcox is a general partner at Pillar, a seed stage VC firm in Boston. Russ comes to VC after a career as a serial entrepreneur. He is an Ernst &amp; Young New England Entrepreneur of the Year, a Mass High Tech All-Star, a Venture Partner at the Harvard Business School, and an instructor on entrepreneurship for Harvard SEAS. He was previously CEO of E Ink, a company he co-founded to commercialize electronic paper materials invented at the MIT Media Lab. E Ink conducted ground-breaking research and shipped tens of millions of displays for the Amazon Kindle, SONY Reader, B&amp;N Nook and other devices. In 2009 he sold the company for $480 million. Russ also co-founded Transatomic Power, a project to develop safer and cleaner nuclear energy, and Piper Therapeutics, pursuing a preclinical target for immuno-oncology. He graduated with honors in Applied Mathematics from Harvard College and was a Baker Scholar at Harvard Business School.<\/p>","cbAvatar":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/02\/Russ-Wilcox.jpg","company":"Pillar VC","featured":0,"name":"Russ Wilcox","uuid":"b4049352163a9d7324532abedb51166e","cb_tag":576788887,"position":"Partner","links":{"crunchbase":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.crunchbase.com\/person\/russ-wilcox","twitter":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/http:\/\/twitter.com\/russ_wilcox","linkedin":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231202000540\/https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/in\/russ-wilcox-2005\/"}}],"sponsorships":[{"name":"Silver","level":"tier1","sponsors":[{"address":{"city":"","country":"","state":"","street1":"","street2":"","zip":""},"boothId":"","categories":[],"content":"The only revenue acceleration platform with everything you need to discover, engage, and connect with your ideal customers, all in one place.\r\n\r\nTap into our network of 1+ billion contacts using 30+ custom filters such as social intent, job postings, company growth, job changes, and much more.\r\n\r\nUse the insights gained from efficient sequencing and parallel dialing to refine your sales strategy.
